name: State Pension
description: Contributory pension paid to individuals who have reached State Pension age and have sufficient National Insurance contribution records. Comprises the new State Pension (from April 2016, full rate Â£221.20/week in 2024-25) and the legacy basic/additional State Pension for those who reached pension age before 6 April 2016.
beneficiaries: 12.7 million recipients (2024-25 estimate)
legal_basis: Pensions Act 2014 (new State Pension); Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 (basic/additional)
notes: Largest single item of UK government expenditure. Protected by the triple lock (rises by the highest of earnings growth, CPI inflation, or 2.5%). Funded from the National Insurance Fund.

name: NHS England Core Revenue Allocation
department: Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC)
description: Core revenue funding for NHS England covering hospital and community health services, primary care (GP contracts, prescribing, dental, ophthalmic), specialised commissioning, and public health services delegated from DHSC. This is the single largest DEL programme in UK government.
beneficiaries: Approximately 56.5 million people registered with NHS GPs in England
legal_basis: National Health Service Act 2006 (as amended by Health and Care Act 2022)
notes: NHS England (merged with NHS Improvement in 2022) allocates funding to 42 Integrated Care Boards. The figure includes DHSC resource DEL transferred to NHSE. Separate from NHS Scotland, NHS Wales, and Health and Social Care NI which are devolved.

name: Debt Interest Payments (National Debt Servicing)
description: Interest and financing costs on UK central government debt, including conventional gilts, index-linked gilts, National Savings & Investments products, and Treasury bills. A significant portion relates to index-linked gilt coupons that rise with RPI inflation.
beneficiaries: Not applicable (payments to gilt holders, institutional investors, NS&I savers)
legal_basis: National Loans Act 1968; Debt Management Office framework
notes: Spiked sharply in 2022-23 due to high RPI inflation increasing index-linked gilt costs. The figure is net of the Bank of England Asset Purchase Facility (APF) remittances. Managed by the Debt Management Office under HM Treasury.

name: Dedicated Schools Grant (DSG)
description: Ring-fenced grant to local authorities in England for funding mainstream schools, academies, and free schools. Comprises four blocks: Schools Block (~Â£45B), High Needs Block (~Â£10B for SEND), Early Years Block (~Â£4B), and Central Schools Services Block.
beneficiaries: Approximately 8.9 million pupils in state-funded schools in England
legal_basis: School Standards and Framework Act 1998; School Finance (England) Regulations
notes: Distributed via the National Funding Formula (NFF) introduced from 2018-19. The High Needs Block has faced persistent overspend pressure from rising SEND demand. Academies receive their DSG allocation directly via ESFA.

name: Universal Credit
description: Means-tested benefit replacing six legacy benefits (income-based JSA, income-related ESA, Income Support, Housing Benefit, Child Tax Credit, Working Tax Credit) into a single monthly payment for working-age people on low income or out of work.
beneficiaries: Approximately 6.0 million households on Universal Credit (2024-25)
legal_basis: Welfare Reform Act 2012
notes: Full service rolled out nationally by December 2018; managed migration from legacy benefits ongoing until 2028. Administered through Jobcentre Plus. Standard allowance plus elements for housing, children, disability, childcare, and carer responsibilities.

name: Personal Independence Payment (PIP)
description: Non-means-tested, non-contributory benefit for working-age adults (16-State Pension age) with long-term health conditions or disabilities to help with extra costs of daily living and mobility. Assessed via functional capability rather than diagnosis.
beneficiaries: Approximately 3.5 million claimants (2024-25)
legal_basis: Welfare Reform Act 2012, Part 4
notes: Replaced Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for new working-age claimants from April 2013. Two components: Daily Living (Â£72.65 or Â£108.55/week) and Mobility (Â£28.70 or Â£75.75/week). Assessments conducted by contracted providers (Capita, Atos/IAS). Fastest-growing area of welfare spending.

name: Housing Benefit
description: Means-tested benefit to help tenants on low incomes pay their rent, covering both social housing and private rented sector (subject to Local Housing Allowance rates). Being gradually replaced by the housing element of Universal Credit.
beneficiaries: Approximately 2.8 million claimants (declining as UC migration proceeds)
legal_basis: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992; Housing Benefit Regulations 2006
notes: Administered by local authorities on behalf of DWP. Legacy Housing Benefit will be fully replaced by UC housing costs element by 2028. Pensioner claimants on Pension Credit remain on Housing Benefit and will not migrate to UC.

name: Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
description: Benefit for people with a health condition or disability that limits their ability to work. Comes in two forms: contributory (based on NI record, now called 'new style' ESA) and income-related (legacy, being replaced by UC). Claimants placed in Support Group or Work-Related Activity Group.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.7 million claimants (declining due to UC migration)
legal_basis: Welfare Reform Act 2007
notes: Replaced Incapacity Benefit and Income Support paid on grounds of incapacity. Income-related ESA is a legacy benefit being migrated to UC. Contributory (new style) ESA continues alongside UC. Assessed via the Work Capability Assessment.

name: Pension Credit
description: Means-tested top-up benefit for pensioners on low incomes, guaranteeing a minimum weekly income (Guarantee Credit, Â£218.15/week for singles in 2024-25). Also includes a Savings Credit element rewarding modest retirement savings for those who reached pension age before April 2016.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.4 million claimant households (significant under-claiming estimated at ~850,000 eligible non-claimants)
legal_basis: State Pension Credit Act 2002
notes: Acts as a passport to other benefits including Housing Benefit, Council Tax Reduction, Cold Weather Payments, and free TV licences (for over-75s). DWP actively campaigns to increase take-up given estimated 40% non-claiming rate.

name: Attendance Allowance
description: Non-means-tested, tax-free benefit for people of State Pension age and above who have a physical or mental disability requiring frequent attention or supervision. Paid at two rates: lower (Â£72.65/week daytime or nighttime needs) and higher (Â£108.55/week both).
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.6 million claimants
legal_basis: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992, Section 64
notes: Unlike PIP (working-age equivalent), there is no mobility component. No formal medical assessment visit in most cases â€” decided on paper-based evidence. Not affected by UC migration. Devolved to Scotland as Pension Age Disability Payment from 2025.

name: Child Benefit
description: Universal (non-means-tested) payment to parents or guardians responsible for a child under 16 (or under 20 in approved education/training). Paid at Â£25.60/week for eldest child and Â£16.95/week for each additional child (2024-25 rates).
beneficiaries: Approximately 7.0 million families covering 12.7 million children
legal_basis: Child Benefit Act 2005 (consolidating earlier legislation from Family Allowances Act 1945)
notes: Subject to the High Income Child Benefit Charge (HICBC) for households where a parent earns over Â£60,000 (threshold raised from Â£50,000 in April 2024). Also confers National Insurance credits to the claiming parent. Replaced Family Allowances (1945) and was originally under DSS.

name: Tax Credits (Working Tax Credit and Child Tax Credit)
description: Means-tested income supplements for working people on low incomes (Working Tax Credit) and families with children (Child Tax Credit). Legacy benefits being replaced by Universal Credit through managed migration.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.5 million households (rapidly declining from peak of 4.4 million in 2012)
legal_basis: Tax Credits Act 2002
notes: Administered by HMRC (unlike most benefits which are DWP). Managed migration to UC scheduled for completion by March 2028. During transition, claimants receive transitional protection so they are no worse off at point of transfer. Annual renewals required.

name: Police Core Grant and Formula Funding
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£10 billion (central grants; total police funding ~Â£18B including precept)
description: Central government grants to the 43 territorial police forces in England and Wales, comprising the Police Core Grant, formula funding, and various specific grants (counter-terrorism, serious violence, uplift). Supplemented by local council tax police precept.
beneficiaries: 56.5 million people in England and Wales served by 43 police forces (~150,000 officers)
legal_basis: Police Act 1996; Police Reform and Social Responsibility Act 2011
notes: Distributed via the Police Funding Formula (under review). The 20,000 officer uplift programme was completed in 2023. Police and Crime Commissioners set local precept levels within referendum limits. Counter-terrorism policing grant (~Â£1B) is ring-fenced.

name: Scottish Government Block Grant
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£45 billion (total Scottish Government Fiscal Framework)
description: Block grant from HM Treasury to the Scottish Government, determined by the Barnett Formula. Funds devolved services including NHS Scotland, Scottish education, justice, transport, and local government. Adjusted for tax devolution under the Scotland Act 2016 fiscal framework.
beneficiaries: 5.4 million people in Scotland
legal_basis: Scotland Act 1998 (as amended by Scotland Act 2012 and Scotland Act 2016)
notes: The Barnett Formula provides population-share-based increments to the block grant when English/UK spending changes. Since 2017 the grant is adjusted for devolved tax revenues (Scottish Income Tax, LBTT, Scottish Landfill Tax). The fiscal framework includes a Block Grant Adjustment mechanism.

name: Welsh Government Block Grant
description: Block grant from HM Treasury to the Welsh Government, determined by the Barnett Formula with a needs-based floor introduced in 2015. Funds devolved services including NHS Wales, Welsh education, local government, housing, and transport within Wales.
beneficiaries: 3.1 million people in Wales
legal_basis: Government of Wales Act 2006 (as amended by Wales Act 2014 and Wales Act 2017)
notes: The 2015 funding floor guarantees Welsh funding at 115% of comparable English spending per head. Limited tax devolution includes Welsh Rates of Income Tax (from April 2019) and Land Transaction Tax. The Senedd (Welsh Parliament) sets devolved tax rates.

name: Northern Ireland Block Grant
description: Block grant from HM Treasury to the Northern Ireland Executive, determined by the Barnett Formula. Funds devolved services including Health and Social Care NI, education, policing (PSNI), justice, and infrastructure. Northern Ireland has the widest devolution settlement in the UK.
beneficiaries: 1.9 million people in Northern Ireland
legal_basis: Northern Ireland Act 1998 (as amended); Stormont House Agreement; New Decade New Approach (2020)
notes: NI devolution includes policing, justice, and social security (though in practice NI largely mirrors GB benefit rates via parity convention). Spending per head is significantly above the UK average. Fiscal instability due to periodic Assembly suspensions (2017-20, 2022-24).

name: MOD Military Personnel Costs
description: Pay, allowances, pensions contributions, and associated personnel costs for UK regular and reserve Armed Forces across the Royal Navy, British Army, and Royal Air Force. Includes military salaries, X-Factor supplement, operational allowances, and employer pension contributions to AFPS.
beneficiaries: Approximately 145,000 regular Armed Forces personnel plus 27,000 volunteer reserves
legal_basis: Armed Forces Act 2006 (renewed quinquennially); Armed Forces Pay Review Body recommendations
notes: Personnel costs represent roughly 30% of the total MOD budget (~Â£55B in 2024-25). Armed Forces pension schemes (AFPS 75, 05, 15) are unfunded public service pension schemes. The Armed Forces Pay Review Body makes independent pay recommendations to the Prime Minister.

name: MOD Equipment and Support (Defence Procurement)
department: Ministry of Defence (MOD) / Defence Equipment and Support (DE&S)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£22 billion (equipment plan total ~Â£290B over 10 years)
description: Capital and resource expenditure on military equipment procurement, support, and maintenance managed through Defence Equipment & Support (DE&S). Covers major programmes including Dreadnought-class submarines, Type 26 frigates, F-35B Lightning, Ajax AFVs, and Tempest/GCAP future combat air.
beneficiaries: Not applicable (national defence capability)
legal_basis: Government procurement regulations; Defence and Security Public Contracts Regulations 2011
notes: The 10-year Equipment Plan is reviewed annually by the NAO, which has consistently found an affordability gap. DE&S (based in Abbey Wood, Bristol) employs ~12,500 staff. The 2023 Defence Command Paper Refresh committed to 2.5% GDP on defence. AUKUS submarine programme is the single largest procurement commitment.

name: Network Rail Government Grant
description: Government grant to Network Rail (reclassified as a public body in 2014) to fund operations, maintenance, and renewals of the railway infrastructure in England and Wales â€” including 20,000 miles of track, 30,000 bridges, and 2,500 stations.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.7 billion passenger journeys per year on the GB rail network
legal_basis: Railways Act 1993 (as amended); reclassified to public sector 2014; Great British Railways (Transition) in progress
notes: Network Rail's funding is set in 5-year Control Periods by the Office of Rail and Road (ORR). CP7 (2024-29) has a total funding envelope of ~Â£44B. Network Rail is to be absorbed into Great British Railways (GBR), though the transition has been delayed. Separate from ScotRail infrastructure funding via Transport Scotland.

name: High Speed 2 (HS2) Construction Programme
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£6â€“8 billion (peak construction spend, varies by year)
description: Capital funding for the construction of the UK's high-speed railway. Phase 1 runs from London Euston to Birmingham Curzon Street (scheduled to open 2029-33). The Phase 2 extensions to Manchester and Leeds were cancelled in October 2023, with funding redirected to Network North.
beneficiaries: Not applicable until operational (estimated 300,000+ daily passengers at full operation)
legal_basis: High Speed Rail (London - West Midlands) Act 2017; High Speed Rail (West Midlands - Crewe) Act 2021 (now paused)
notes: Total estimated cost of Phase 1 revised to Â£54-66 billion (2023 prices). HS2 Ltd is the arm's-length delivery body. Phase 2b (Crewe to Manchester) cancelled October 2023 by PM Sunak; Network North programme announced as alternative. The Euston station scope has been reduced.

name: Revenue Support Grant (RSG) and Local Government Finance Settlement
department: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£3 billion (RSG alone; total MHCLG local govt funding ~Â£12B including business rates redistribution)
description: Un-ring-fenced central government revenue grant to local authorities in England to support core services. Part of the wider Local Government Finance Settlement which also includes redistributed business rates, specific grants, and the New Homes Bonus.
beneficiaries: 317 local authorities in England serving approximately 56.5 million residents
legal_basis: Local Government Finance Act 1988; Local Government Finance Act 1992
notes: RSG has been dramatically reduced since 2010 austerity â€” many councils now receive zero RSG. The Fair Funding Review (to update the needs-based distribution formula) has been repeatedly delayed since 2016. Social care grant, Services Grant, and other specific grants now comprise a larger share of settlement than RSG.

name: Affordable Homes Programme (AHP)
department: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) / Homes England
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£2.4 billion per year (2021-26 programme: Â£11.5B total)
description: Capital grant programme delivered through Homes England and the Greater London Authority to fund the construction of new affordable housing in England â€” including affordable rent, social rent, shared ownership, and rent-to-buy homes.
beneficiaries: Target of approximately 180,000 new affordable homes over the 2021-26 programme
legal_basis: Housing and Regeneration Act 2008 (establishing Homes England, formerly HCA)
notes: The 2021-26 AHP totals Â£11.5 billion, the largest single affordable housing investment in a decade. Homes England partners with housing associations and local authorities. Affordable Rent is capped at 80% of local market rent; the programme increasingly includes Social Rent tenure. Separate from Right to Buy receipts and Section 106 contributions.

name: NHS Capital Investment (DHSC Capital DEL)
department: Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) / NHS England
description: Capital expenditure on NHS infrastructure in England including new hospital construction (New Hospital Programme), medical equipment, digital and IT (including NHS App, electronic patient records), estates maintenance and backlog reduction, and ambulance fleet.
beneficiaries: 56.5 million NHS users in England across ~1,200 NHS sites
legal_basis: National Health Service Act 2006; Health and Care Act 2022
notes: The New Hospital Programme (originally '40 new hospitals by 2030') has been rescoped and delayed â€” NAO found the timeline and budget unrealistic. RAAC (reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete) remediation is consuming a significant portion of capital budgets. NHS digital transformation includes Federated Data Platform.

name: UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA, formerly Public Health England)
department: Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£0.5 billion (UKHSA core); Public Health Grant to LAs ~Â£3.6B
description: Executive agency responsible for protecting the nation from health threats including infectious disease surveillance, outbreak response, immunisation programmes, chemical and radiation hazards. Replaced Public Health England (PHE, est. 2013) following the COVID-19 pandemic review.
beneficiaries: 67 million UK population (health protection is UK-wide for some functions)
legal_basis: Established as executive agency of DHSC (April 2021); no primary legislation â€” operates under DHSC framework
notes: PHE was dissolved in October 2021, with health protection functions going to UKHSA and health improvement functions transferred to the Office for Health Improvement and Disparities (OHID) within DHSC and to local authorities via the Public Health Grant. Porton Down and Colindale are key UKHSA science sites.

name: Legal Aid (Civil and Criminal)
department: Ministry of Justice (MOJ) / Legal Aid Agency (LAA)
description: Publicly funded legal advice and representation for individuals who cannot afford lawyers, covering criminal defence (duty solicitors, Crown Court advocacy) and civil matters (family, housing, immigration, mental health, debt). Administered by the Legal Aid Agency.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.5 million legal aid acts of assistance per year
legal_basis: Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 (LASPO)
notes: LASPO 2012 drastically reduced civil legal aid scope â€” removing most private family law, clinical negligence, employment, and welfare benefit cases. Criminal legal aid has faced sustainability concerns due to low fee rates. The 2021 Independent Review of Criminal Legal Aid (Bellamy Review) recommended 15% fee increase. Means test reform under the Criminal Legal Aid Review.

name: HM Prison Service (HM Prison and Probation Service â€” Prison Operations)
description: Operational costs of running 117 prisons in England and Wales, including staff pay, maintenance, prisoner healthcare (commissioned via NHS England), education, food, and security. Managed by HM Prison and Probation Service (HMPPS), an executive agency of MOJ.
beneficiaries: Approximately 87,000 prisoners (operational capacity ~89,000 in 2024-25)
legal_basis: Prison Act 1952; Offender Management Act 2007
notes: Persistent overcrowding â€” prison population near operational capacity. New prison construction programme (including HMP Five Wells, HMP Fosse Way) adding ~10,000 places. Some prisons privately managed (Serco, G4S, Sodexo). HMPPS was formed in 2017 by merging the National Offender Management Service (NOMS) functions.

name: Probation Service
description: Supervision and rehabilitation of offenders in the community, including managing community sentences, licence supervision of released prisoners, pre-sentence reports, approved premises (hostels), and electronic monitoring. Returned to full public ownership in 2021.
beneficiaries: Approximately 250,000 offenders under probation supervision at any time
legal_basis: Offender Management Act 2007; Offender Rehabilitation Act 2014
notes: Chris Grayling's 2014 Transforming Rehabilitation programme outsourced probation to 21 Community Rehabilitation Companies (CRCs). This was widely judged a failure (HMI Probation, NAO) and reversed in June 2021, reunifying probation under HMPPS. The Probation Service now employs ~18,000 staff across 12 regions.

name: Border Force
description: Law enforcement command within the Home Office responsible for immigration and customs controls at UK ports and airports, including passport checks, customs enforcement, detection of prohibited goods, and small boat interceptions in the English Channel.
beneficiaries: Not directly applicable (processes ~250 million passenger arrivals per year at UK borders)
legal_basis: Immigration Act 1971; Borders, Citizenship and Immigration Act 2009; UK Border Act 2007
notes: Created in 2012 when the UK Border Agency was split into Border Force (operational) and UKVI (casework). Around 9,000 staff operate at 140+ ports and airports. ePassport gates, Advance Passenger Information, and the Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) scheme are key capabilities. Small boats Channel crossings have driven increased operational demand.

name: Counter-Terrorism Policing and CONTEST Programme
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£1.1 billion (CT policing grant + Prevent + Protect/Prepare)
description: Funding for the UK's counter-terrorism strategy (CONTEST) comprising four strands: Prevent (deradicalisation), Pursue (investigation and disruption), Protect (infrastructure security), and Prepare (emergency response). Includes CT policing grant to forces, MI5 interface, and Channel programme.
beneficiaries: 67 million UK population (national security function)
legal_basis: Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015; Terrorism Act 2000; Counter-Terrorism and Border Security Act 2019
notes: CT policing is coordinated nationally by Counter Terrorism Policing (CTP) led by the Met's SO15. MI5 (Security Service) budget is separate and classified under the Single Intelligence Account (~Â£4B total for MI5, MI6, GCHQ). The Prevent Duty Review (2023, Shawcross) led to programme reforms. Martyn's Law (Protect Duty) introduces venue security obligations.

name: Official Development Assistance (ODA) â€” FCDO Programme Spend
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£8 billion (FCDO bilateral/multilateral ODA; total UK ODA ~Â£15B across all departments)
description: UK bilateral and multilateral overseas development assistance, funding programmes in global health, education, humanitarian response, climate finance, governance, and economic development. Includes contributions to multilateral organisations (World Bank IDA, Global Fund, Gavi, UN agencies).
beneficiaries: Hundreds of millions of people across partner countries (UK bilateral ODA reaches 100+ countries)
legal_basis: International Development Act 2002; International Development (Official Development Assistance Target) Act 2015
notes: The ODA target was cut from 0.7% to 0.5% of GNI in 2021 (fiscal consolidation). The 2015 Act enshrines the 0.7% target in law but permits temporary deviation. DFID merged with FCO to form FCDO in September 2020. FCDO administers ~60% of UK ODA; remainder sits across 15+ other departments (DHSC, BEIS, MOD, Home Office â€” notably in-country refugee costs count as ODA).

name: BBC World Service Grant-in-Aid
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£104 million (FCDO grant-in-aid; total WS budget ~Â£300M including BBC licence fee)
description: Government grant to the BBC World Service for international broadcasting in over 40 languages, providing independent news and analysis to global audiences. FCDO funding supplements BBC licence fee funding (which became the primary funder from 2014).
beneficiaries: Estimated weekly global audience of 365 million people across radio, TV, and digital
legal_basis: BBC Royal Charter and Agreement (2017-2027); Broadcasting Act 1981 (original basis)
notes: Until 2014, the World Service was entirely funded by FCO/FCDO grant-in-aid. The 2010 Spending Review transferred primary funding responsibility to the BBC licence fee, with FCDO retaining a supplementary grant focused on new language services and strategic priorities. Languages include Arabic, Persian, Hindi, Hausa, and Somali.

name: Environment Agency
department: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra)
description: Executive non-departmental public body responsible for environmental regulation in England, including flood risk management, water quality and resources, waste regulation, fisheries, and industrial emissions permitting. Major capital programme in flood defences.
beneficiaries: 56.5 million people in England (environmental protection) plus 5.2 million properties in flood risk areas
legal_basis: Environment Act 1995; Environment Act 2021; Flood and Water Management Act 2010
notes: The EA is the largest of Defra's arm's-length bodies with ~11,000 staff. A significant portion of income comes from regulatory charges (environmental permits, abstraction licences). The flood defence capital programme is the single largest investment area. Chair and board appointed by Defra Secretary of State.

name: Natural England
department: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra)
description: Government adviser and delivery body for the natural environment in England, responsible for designating and managing SSSIs, National Nature Reserves, AONBs (now National Landscapes), wildlife licensing, Biodiversity Net Gain, and Environmental Land Management scheme advice.
beneficiaries: Not directly applicable (protects 4,100+ SSSIs covering 8% of England's land area)
legal_basis: Natural Environment and Rural Communities Act 2006 (NERC Act)
notes: Replaced English Nature and parts of the Countryside Agency and Rural Development Service. Budget was severely cut during austerity (~40% reduction 2010-2020) affecting regulatory capacity. Key role in Biodiversity Net Gain (mandatory from Feb 2024) and Environmental Land Management (ELM) scheme delivery. ~2,200 staff.

name: Flood and Coastal Erosion Risk Management Programme
department: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) / Environment Agency
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£1.1 billion (2021-27 programme: Â£5.2B total capital)
description: Capital investment programme to build and maintain flood defences, coastal erosion protection, and surface water management infrastructure across England. Delivered primarily by the Environment Agency with contributions from local levy, partnership funding, and local authorities.
beneficiaries: Target of 336,000 properties better protected from flooding over the 2021-27 programme period
legal_basis: Flood and Water Management Act 2010; Environment Act 2021; Coast Protection Act 1949
notes: The 2020 Policy Statement committed Â£5.2B in capital over 2021-27, double the previous programme. Partnership funding (introduced 2011) requires local contributions for schemes to proceed. Climate change projections suggest flood risk will increase significantly â€” the 2024 National Flood Risk Assessment identifies 6.3 million properties at risk.

name: Farming Subsidies (Basic Payment Scheme / Environmental Land Management)
department: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) / Rural Payments Agency
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£2.4 billion (total farming support budget ring-fenced to 2029)
description: Direct payments to farmers in England, transitioning from area-based EU Common Agricultural Policy payments (Basic Payment Scheme) to Environmental Land Management (ELM) schemes that pay for environmental outcomes â€” Sustainable Farming Incentive (SFI), Countryside Stewardship (CS), and Landscape Recovery.
beneficiaries: Approximately 85,000 farm businesses in England receiving BPS; 37,000+ with ELM agreements (and growing)
legal_basis: Agriculture Act 2020; Direct Payments to Farmers (Legislative Continuity) Act 2020
notes: BPS direct payments are being progressively reduced (delinked from 2024, no land required) and phased out by 2027. The freed-up funding is redirected to ELM schemes. SFI uptake has accelerated since simplified 2023 offer. The total annual farming budget of ~Â£2.4B is ring-fenced at the level previously received from the EU CAP. Separate schemes operate in Scotland (Scottish Suckler Beef Support), Wales, and NI.

name: Nuclear Decommissioning Authority (NDA)
department: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ, formerly BEIS)
description: Non-departmental public body responsible for decommissioning and cleaning up the UK's earliest nuclear sites, including Sellafield, Dounreay, Magnox reactor sites, and associated waste management and spent fuel reprocessing. Manages the UK's nuclear legacy over a 100+ year programme.
beneficiaries: Not directly applicable (environmental cleanup and safety; ~17,000 NDA group employees)
legal_basis: Energy Act 2004
notes: The total estimated undiscounted lifetime cost of the NDA mission is approximately Â£132 billion. Sellafield Ltd (the NDA's largest subsidiary) alone accounts for ~Â£2.5B annual spend and is the largest nuclear site in Europe. Key programmes include Magnox reactor defuelling, Sellafield legacy ponds/silos remediation, and the Geological Disposal Facility (GDF) for higher-activity waste.

name: UK Research and Innovation (UKRI)
department: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT, formerly BEIS)
description: Umbrella body for the UK's seven research councils (EPSRC, BBSRC, MRC, NERC, AHRC, ESRC, STFC), Innovate UK, and Research England. Funds university research grants, fellowships, research infrastructure, innovation partnerships, and the UK's share of Horizon Europe association.
beneficiaries: Approximately 90,000 researchers funded; 160+ UK universities and research institutes
legal_basis: Higher Education and Research Act 2017
notes: UKRI was formed in April 2018 to bring together research funding under one strategic body. Total budget includes QR (Quality-Related) block grant funding to universities via Research England. UK associated to Horizon Europe from January 2024 (after prolonged Brexit-related exclusion). UKRI also manages ARIA (Advanced Research + Invention Agency, est. 2022) relationship.

name: Student Loans Company (SLC) Operations and Student Finance
oscar_category: annually managed expenditure (AME) for loan outlay; DEL for administration
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£20 billion (new loan outlay per year; RAB charge ~45%)
description: Government-owned company that administers student finance in the UK â€” processing applications, disbursing tuition fee loans and maintenance loans to higher education students in England (and administering devolved schemes for Wales, NI, Scotland). Also handles income-contingent loan repayment collection via HMRC PAYE.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.8 million students receiving loans annually; total loan book ~Â£236 billion (2024)
legal_basis: Education (Student Loans) Act 1990; Teaching and Higher Education Act 1998; Higher Education and Research Act 2017
notes: The outstanding student loan book exceeds Â£236 billion. Under Plan 5 (from Sept 2023), the repayment threshold is Â£25,000 with a 40-year repayment term. The Resource Accounting and Budgeting (RAB) charge â€” the estimated proportion of loans not repaid â€” is approximately 45%. SLC employs ~4,500 staff, primarily in Darlington and Glasgow.

name: Early Years Entitlements (Free Childcare Hours)
oscar_category: departmental expenditure limits (DEL, via DSG Early Years Block)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£5 billion (rising to ~Â£8B by 2027-28 with expansion)
description: Funded early education and childcare entitlements for children in England: universal 15 hours/week for all 3-4 year olds; extended 30 hours for working parents of 3-4 year olds; 15 hours for disadvantaged 2 year olds; and new entitlements expanding from April 2024 to working parents of 9 months+.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.7 million children taking up funded places (rising significantly with expansion to under-2s)
legal_basis: Childcare Act 2006; Childcare Act 2016 (30 hours extension)
notes: Major expansion announced Spring Budget 2023: 15 hours for working parents of 2-year-olds from April 2024, 15 hours from 9 months from September 2024, and 30 hours from 9 months from September 2025. Funding rates to providers have been significantly increased. Delivered through maintained nurseries, private/voluntary/independent (PVI) settings, and childminders.

name: Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND) Provision
oscar_category: departmental expenditure limits (DEL, via DSG High Needs Block)
description: Funding for children and young people aged 0-25 with special educational needs and disabilities, covering Education Health and Care Plans (EHCPs), specialist provision in special schools and alternative provision, SEN support in mainstream schools, and post-16 high needs places.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.6 million children with SEN (including 576,000 with EHCPs as of January 2024)
legal_basis: Children and Families Act 2014, Part 3; SEND Code of Practice 2015
notes: SEND is the most financially pressured area of education spending. EHCP numbers have risen 80%+ since 2015. The cumulative DSG deficit across local authorities exceeds Â£4 billion. The 2023 SEND and AP Improvement Plan proposed reforms but was widely criticised as insufficient. A statutory override prevents DSG deficits from technically bankrupting councils (extended to 2026).

name: 16-19 Education Funding (Post-16 Revenue Funding)
department: Department for Education (DfE) / Education and Skills Funding Agency (ESFA)
description: Revenue funding for post-16 education in England, covering sixth form colleges, school sixth forms, further education (FE) colleges, and independent training providers for students aged 16-19 (and 19-24 with EHCPs). Allocated via the 16-19 national funding formula based on student numbers and programme hours.
beneficiaries: Approximately 1.6 million 16-19 year olds in funded education and training
legal_basis: Education Act 2011; Apprenticeships, Skills, Children and Learning Act 2009
notes: The 16-19 base rate was frozen at Â£4,000 per student for nearly a decade before being increased to Â£4,642 in 2023-24. FE colleges have been significantly underfunded relative to schools and universities. T-levels (introduced 2020) are the flagship 16-19 technical qualification, designed as equivalent to 3 A-levels. Condition of further education college estates is a major concern.

name: National Highways (Strategic Road Network)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£6 billion (Road Investment Strategy period average)
description: Government-owned company (formerly Highways England, originally Highways Agency) responsible for operating, maintaining, and improving England's strategic road network â€” approximately 4,500 miles of motorways and major A-roads carrying one-third of all traffic.
beneficiaries: Approximately 4 million vehicle journeys per day on the strategic road network
legal_basis: Infrastructure Act 2015; Road Investment Strategy
notes: Funded through Road Investment Strategy (RIS) periods: RIS2 (2020-25) committed Â£27.4B. National Highways manages major schemes including the A303 Stonehenge tunnel (approved), Lower Thames Crossing (DCO resubmitted), and smart motorway programme (paused in 2023 following safety concerns). Maintenance backlog is a growing issue.

name: Bus Subsidies and Funding (BSIP and BSOG)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£1.5 billion (including BSOG, BSIP, Â£2 fare cap, and concessionary fares reimbursement)
description: Central government support for bus services in England, comprising Bus Service Operators Grant (BSOG â€” fuel duty rebate), Bus Service Improvement Plans (BSIP) funding, the national Â£2 single fare cap, and reimbursement to operators for statutory concessionary travel (free bus passes for over-66s and disabled people).
beneficiaries: Approximately 3.4 billion bus passenger journeys per year in England; 10 million concessionary pass holders
legal_basis: Transport Act 1985; Transport Act 2000; Bus Services Act 2017; Concessionary Bus Travel Act 2007
notes: The National Bus Strategy (Bus Back Better, 2021) required all local transport authorities to produce BSIPs. The Â£2 single fare cap (introduced Jan 2023) was extended to December 2025. Bus patronage remains below pre-COVID levels (~85% of 2019). Franchising powers (Bus Services Act 2017) are being pursued by several mayoral combined authorities following Greater Manchester's lead.

name: Arts Council England
department: Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS)
description: National development agency for arts, museums, and libraries in England, distributing government grant-in-aid and National Lottery funds. Provides regular funding to National Portfolio Organisations (NPOs), project grants, and strategic funding for cultural infrastructure, talent development, and community arts.
beneficiaries: 985 National Portfolio Organisations funded 2023-26; thousands of individual artists via project grants; ~150 million museum/gallery visits per year
legal_basis: Royal Charter (1946, revised 1967, 2002); National Lottery etc. Act 1993
notes: The 2023-26 Investment Programme was the most significant rebalancing in ACE's history â€” 'Let's Create' strategy shifted ~Â£50M from London organisations to under-served areas, with some high-profile London organisations (English National Opera, Donmar Warehouse) losing NPO status. ACE also funds national museums' purchase grants and Music Education Hubs.

name: British Council
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£0.2 billion (FCDO grant-in-aid; total BC turnover ~Â£1.1B including self-generated income)
description: UK's international organisation for cultural relations and educational opportunities, operating in 100+ countries. Delivers English language teaching, educational exchanges, arts and culture programmes, exams administration (IELTS partner), and supports UK soft power objectives.
beneficiaries: Approximately 75 million people engaged directly across 100+ countries annually
legal_basis: Royal Charter (1940); operates as executive NDPB of FCDO
notes: Only ~18% of income comes from government grant; the majority is self-generated from English language testing (IELTS, jointly owned with Cambridge), exams delivery, and teaching. Significant restructuring since 2020 â€” closed offices in multiple countries and reduced headcount. Plays a key role in Chevening Scholarships administration (FCDO funded).

name: UK Export Finance (UKEF)
department: Department for Business and Trade (DBT, formerly DIT)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£0.1 billion (net operating cost, but maximum exposure ~Â£67B)
description: UK's official export credit agency providing government-backed loans, guarantees, and insurance to support British exports and overseas investment. Products include buyer credit, direct lending, export insurance, and bond support schemes to help UK exporters win contracts in challenging markets.
beneficiaries: Approximately 600 exporters supported per year; maximum portfolio exposure of Â£67 billion
legal_basis: Export and Investment Guarantees Act 1991; UKEF framework document
notes: UKEF typically operates at no net cost to the taxpayer â€” it generates a surplus in most years through premium income exceeding claims. Originally the Export Credits Guarantee Department (ECGD), rebranded as UKEF in 2011. Has supported major transactions including Rolls-Royce engine exports, defence contracts, and renewable energy projects. Financial capacity increased to Â£67B.

name: Companies House
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£0.09 billion (transitioning from fee-funded to grant-funded model)
description: Executive agency responsible for incorporating and dissolving companies in the UK, maintaining the public register of companies, and ensuring compliance with filing requirements (annual accounts, confirmation statements, persons with significant control). Registers approximately 800,000 new companies per year.
beneficiaries: 5.2 million companies on the UK register; public access to company data
legal_basis: Companies Act 2006; Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023
notes: The Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023 gives Companies House significant new powers: identity verification for directors and PSCs, power to query and reject filings, and stronger data sharing with law enforcement. This represents the biggest transformation in Companies House's 180-year history. Based in Cardiff with ~1,200 staff. Filing data is freely available on the register.

name: Ordnance Survey
department: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£0.18 billion (turnover â€” largely self-funded via data licensing and product sales)
description: National mapping agency of Great Britain, producing and licensing the definitive geospatial data and mapping for the country. Products include OS MasterMap, AddressBase, OS Maps for leisure, and the National Geographic Database. Operating as a government trading fund and public corporation.
beneficiaries: UK public sector (free access via PSGA licence), 4 million leisure map users, commercial licensees
legal_basis: Ordnance Survey Trading Fund Order 1999; operates under a Framework Document with DSIT
notes: Ordnance Survey is one of the world's oldest national mapping agencies (founded 1791 for military mapping). Operates as a government trading fund â€” required to be self-financing with a target return on capital. The Public Sector Geospatial Agreement (PSGA) provides free access to OS data for all UK public sector bodies. Headquartered in Southampton. Separate from Ordnance Survey Northern Ireland.

name: Met Office
department: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT, formerly BEIS)
approximate_annual_spend_gbp: Â£0.27 billion (turnover â€” mix of Public Weather Service funding + commercial income)
description: UK's national meteorological service providing weather forecasts, climate research, and severe weather warnings. Operates the Public Weather Service (government-funded) alongside commercial weather services for aviation, defence, energy, media, and international clients. World-leading climate modelling centre.
beneficiaries: 67 million UK population (weather warnings and forecasts); global research community; aviation/defence clients
legal_basis: Met Office Trading Fund Order 1996; operates under a Framework Document with DSIT
notes: Operates as a government trading fund based in Exeter. The Public Weather Service is directly funded by DSIT (~Â£120M). Operates one of the world's most powerful supercomputers for weather and climate modelling (new Â£1.2B Microsoft partnership for next-gen supercomputer announced 2023). Provides official climate projections (UKCP) and is a WMO designated centre. Defence weather services provided under contract to MOD.

name: Crown Prosecution Service (CPS)
description: Principal public prosecution service for England and Wales, responsible for reviewing and prosecuting criminal cases investigated by the police and other agencies. Applies the Code for Crown Prosecutors (evidential and public interest tests) to decide whether to charge and prosecute.
beneficiaries: Not directly applicable (criminal justice function serving 56.5 million people in England and Wales; handles ~400,000 defendants per year)
legal_basis: Prosecution of Offences Act 1985
notes: The CPS is independent of police and government â€” prosecution decisions are made free from political influence. Headed by the Director of Public Prosecutions (DPP), who is superintended by the Attorney General. Organised into 14 geographic Areas plus specialist casework divisions (fraud, terrorism, special crime, organised crime). Employs ~6,500 staff including ~3,000 prosecutors. Separate from the Serious Fraud Office and service prosecution authorities.

name: Jobcentre Plus / DWP Operations
description: Executive agency of the Department for Work and Pensions that administers working-age welfare benefits and provides employment support services. Formed by merging the Employment Service and the Benefits Agency to create a single point of contact for benefit claimants and job seekers.
beneficiaries: Working-age adults seeking employment, benefit claimants (JSA, UC), employers recruiting staff

name: Child Maintenance Group / Child Maintenance Service
description: Replaced the Child Support Agency to arrange and enforce child maintenance payments between separated parents. Operates statutory calculation and collection services where family-based arrangements are not possible.
beneficiaries: Children of separated parents, receiving parents (typically resident parents)

name: Disability Living Allowance (DLA) — Legacy
description: Non-means-tested benefit for people with disabilities who need help with personal care or mobility, introduced by the Disability Living Allowance and Disability Working Allowance Act 1991. Now closed to new working-age claimants (replaced by Personal Independence Payment from 2013) but still paid to existing claimants and children under 16.
beneficiaries: Disabled individuals (legacy claimants), disabled children under 16

name: Bereavement Benefits (DWP)
description: Bereavement Support Payment replaced the previous Bereavement Allowance, Widowed Parent's Allowance, and Bereavement Payment for deaths from April 2017 onwards. Provides a lump sum and up to 18 monthly instalments to surviving spouses or civil partners below State Pension age.
beneficiaries: Surviving spouses and civil partners of deceased persons who paid National Insurance contributions

name: Industrial Injuries Benefits
description: Scheme providing Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit (IIDB) and related supplements to employees who are disabled as a result of an accident at work or a prescribed industrial disease. Established under the National Insurance (Industrial Injuries) Act 1946 as part of the post-war welfare state.
beneficiaries: Workers disabled by workplace accidents or prescribed occupational diseases

name: Winter Fuel Payment
description: Annual tax-free payment to help older people pay for heating during winter, introduced by the incoming Labour government. Typically paid automatically to eligible recipients in November or December each year.
beneficiaries: Individuals who have reached State Pension age (eligibility tightened from winter 2024/25 to Pension Credit recipients only)

name: Cold Weather Payment
description: Automatic payment of £25 for each seven-day period of very cold weather (average temperature recorded or forecast to be 0°C or below) in a claimant's area. Linked to specific qualifying benefits including Pension Credit, Income Support, and income-based JSA/ESA.
beneficiaries: Recipients of qualifying means-tested benefits during periods of severe cold weather

name: Budgeting Loans / Budgeting Advances
description: Interest-free loans from the Social Fund (Budgeting Loans for legacy benefits) or Budgeting Advances (for Universal Credit claimants) to help with intermittent expenses such as furniture, clothing, or moving costs. Repaid through deductions from ongoing benefit payments.
beneficiaries: Benefit recipients on low incomes facing one-off or intermittent costs

name: Carer's Allowance
description: Benefit for people who spend at least 35 hours a week caring for someone receiving a qualifying disability benefit, originally introduced as Invalid Care Allowance. It is the principal income-replacement benefit for unpaid carers and is one of the lowest-value benefits in the UK system.
beneficiaries: Unpaid carers aged 16 or over providing substantial regular care to a disabled person

name: Statutory Sick Pay (SSP)
description: Minimum level of sick pay that employers are legally required to pay to eligible employees who are too ill to work, introduced by the Social Security and Housing Benefits Act 1982. Paid for up to 28 weeks in a period of incapacity for work.
beneficiaries: Employees who are unable to work due to illness and meet minimum earnings and notification requirements

name: Statutory Maternity Pay / Statutory Paternity Pay
description: Statutory Maternity Pay (SMP, introduced 1987) provides up to 39 weeks of pay to qualifying employed mothers; Statutory Paternity Pay (SPP, introduced 2003) provides up to two weeks for fathers or partners. Paid by employers who can reclaim most or all of the cost from HMRC.
beneficiaries: Employed mothers (SMP) and fathers/partners (SPP) of newborn or newly adopted children

name: Sure Start / Children's Centres
description: Government programme launched to give every child the best possible start in life by bringing together early education, childcare, health, and family support services in disadvantaged areas. Sure Start Children's Centres were rolled out nationally from 2004 but many have since closed or been repurposed due to local authority funding reductions.
beneficiaries: Young children (0–5 years) and their families, particularly in disadvantaged communities

name: Free School Meals Funding
description: Government funding enabling state-funded schools to provide free meals to pupils from low-income families, originating from the Education (Provision of Meals) Act 1906. Currently covers all infant pupils (Reception to Year 2) universally and older pupils whose families receive qualifying benefits.
beneficiaries: School-age children from low-income families; all infant pupils in state schools (universal infant FSM since 2014)

name: Pupil Premium
description: Additional funding allocated to state-funded schools in England for each pupil who is or has been eligible for free school meals, is looked after, or has a parent in the armed forces. Schools have autonomy in how they spend the premium but must demonstrate impact on closing attainment gaps.
beneficiaries: Disadvantaged pupils, looked-after children, and children from service families in state-funded schools

name: Academy Trust Funding
description: Direct funding from the Education and Skills Funding Agency (ESFA) to academy trusts, which are independent state-funded schools operating outside local authority control under a funding agreement with the Secretary of State. Academies were first established under the Learning and Skills Act 2000 and expanded massively from 2010 under the Academies Act.
beneficiaries: Pupils in academy schools across England, multi-academy trusts

name: Further Education Funding (16–19)
description: Funding administered by the Education and Skills Funding Agency for the education and training of 16- to 19-year-olds (and those up to 25 with an Education, Health and Care Plan) in school sixth forms, sixth-form colleges, and further education colleges. Allocated through a national funding formula based on student numbers, programme cost weighting, and disadvantage factors.
beneficiaries: Young people aged 16–19 in post-compulsory education and training in England

name: Apprenticeship Funding (ESFA)
description: Funding for apprenticeship training in England delivered through the apprenticeship levy (paid by employers with pay bills over £3 million) and co-investment from government for smaller employers. Managed by the Education and Skills Funding Agency through digital apprenticeship service accounts.
beneficiaries: Apprentices of all ages, employers offering apprenticeships, approved training providers

name: Office for Students (OfS)
description: Independent regulator of higher education in England, established by the Higher Education and Research Act 2017 to replace the Higher Education Funding Council for England (HEFCE). Regulates university registration, monitors quality and financial sustainability, and administers teaching and access funding.
beneficiaries: Higher education students in England, registered higher education providers

name: UK Atomic Energy Authority (UKAEA)
description: Government research organisation responsible for the development of fusion energy as a safe, sustainable, and low-carbon energy source, established by the Atomic Energy Authority Act 1954. Operates the MAST Upgrade and JET (now decommissioning) fusion experiments at Culham, Oxfordshire.
beneficiaries: UK energy sector, fusion research community, future energy consumers

name: Sellafield Ltd / Nuclear Decommissioning Authority
description: The Nuclear Decommissioning Authority (NDA), established under the Energy Act 2004, is responsible for decommissioning and cleaning up the UK's earliest nuclear sites. Sellafield Ltd is its largest subsidiary, managing the Sellafield site in Cumbria — Europe's most complex nuclear decommissioning challenge, consuming the majority of the NDA's multi-billion pound annual budget.
beneficiaries: Local communities near nuclear sites, the environment, future generations through hazard reduction

name: Defence Equipment & Support (DE&S)
description: Bespoke trading entity of the Ministry of Defence responsible for procuring and supporting all of the UK Armed Forces' equipment, from ships and aircraft to ammunition and rations. Headquartered at MOD Abbey Wood in Bristol with an annual through-put of approximately £20 billion.
beneficiaries: UK Armed Forces (Royal Navy, British Army, Royal Air Force)

name: Defence Science and Technology Laboratory (Dstl)
description: Executive agency of the Ministry of Defence providing specialist science and technology research to support UK defence and security. Headquartered at Porton Down, Wiltshire, with a secondary site at Fort Halstead; delivers capabilities across CBRN defence, cyber, and weapons systems.
beneficiaries: UK Armed Forces, Ministry of Defence, wider UK national security community

name: Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ)
description: UK signals intelligence and information assurance agency, originating as the Government Code and Cypher School in 1919 and renamed GCHQ in 1946. Responsible for providing signals intelligence to government and the Armed Forces, and for protecting government communications, with its public-facing cyber arm the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) established in 2016.
beneficiaries: UK government, Armed Forces, critical national infrastructure operators, general public (via cyber security)

name: Secret Intelligence Service (SIS / MI6)
description: UK's foreign intelligence service responsible for collecting secret intelligence overseas in support of the government's national security, defence, and foreign policy objectives. Placed on a statutory footing by the Intelligence Services Act 1994 and headquartered at Vauxhall Cross, London.
beneficiaries: UK government, national security apparatus, allied intelligence partners

name: Security Service (MI5)
description: UK's domestic counter-intelligence and security agency responsible for protecting the nation against threats including terrorism, espionage, and the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction. Operates under the Security Service Act 1989 and is headquartered at Thames House, London.
beneficiaries: UK government, general public, critical national infrastructure

name: National Crime Agency (NCA)
description: National law enforcement agency tackling serious and organised crime that crosses regional and international borders, established by the Crime and Courts Act 2013 to replace the Serious Organised Crime Agency (SOCA). Leads the UK response to cyber crime, human trafficking, drug trafficking, and economic crime.
beneficiaries: General public, UK law enforcement partners, international crime-fighting agencies

name: Serious Fraud Office (SFO)
description: Specialist prosecuting authority tackling the most complex and serious cases of fraud, bribery, and corruption, established by the Criminal Justice Act 1987. Uniquely combines investigation and prosecution powers in a single organisation and can use Section 2 compulsory powers to obtain documents and testimony.
beneficiaries: Victims of serious fraud and corruption, the financial system, general public

name: Immigration Enforcement
description: Directorate within the Home Office responsible for enforcing immigration law, including removing those with no legal right to remain, disrupting illegal working, and pursuing immigration-related criminality. Formed as a separate command from the former UK Border Agency in 2012.
beneficiaries: General public, lawful migrants (by maintaining system integrity), communities affected by illegal immigration

name: UK Visas and Immigration (UKVI)
description: Part of the Home Office responsible for processing applications from people who want to visit, work, study, or settle in the UK, as well as asylum claims and citizenship applications. Handles millions of visa decisions each year through a global network of visa application centres.
beneficiaries: Visa applicants, migrants, asylum seekers, UK-based sponsors (employers, universities, families)

name: HM Passport Office (HMPO)
description: Executive agency of the Home Office that issues, renews, and manages UK passports, re-established as a standalone agency in 2014 having previously been part of the Identity and Passport Service. Processes approximately 7 million passport applications per year.
beneficiaries: UK citizens and British nationals requiring travel documents

name: Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA)
description: Executive agency of the Department for Transport that maintains the registration and licensing of drivers and vehicles in Great Britain, headquartered in Swansea. Holds over 50 million driver records and 40 million vehicle records and collects approximately £7 billion per year in vehicle excise duty.
beneficiaries: Drivers, vehicle owners, motor trade, law enforcement (via records access)

name: Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA)
description: Executive agency of the Department for Transport formed by the merger of the Driving Standards Agency and the Vehicle and Operator Services Agency. Responsible for conducting driving tests, approving driving instructors, testing vehicles (MOTs), and regulating commercial vehicle operators.
beneficiaries: Learner drivers, driving instructors, vehicle owners, commercial vehicle operators, road users (via road safety)

name: Maritime and Coastguard Agency (MCA)
description: Executive agency of the Department for Transport responsible for implementing the UK's maritime safety policy, co-ordinating search and rescue at sea through HM Coastguard, and preventing coastal pollution. Formed by merging the Coastguard Agency and the Marine Safety Agency.
beneficiaries: Seafarers, coastal communities, recreational water users, the marine environment

name: Civil Aviation Authority (CAA)
description: UK's specialist aviation regulator responsible for safety regulation, airspace policy, consumer protection for air travellers, and economic regulation of airports with significant market power. Established by the Civil Aviation Act 1971 and funded primarily by charges on the aviation industry rather than general taxation.
beneficiaries: Air passengers, airlines, airports, drone operators, general aviation community

name: Office for National Statistics (ONS)
description: UK's largest independent producer of official statistics and the recognised national statistical institute, formed by merging the Central Statistical Office and the Office of Population Censuses and Surveys. Responsible for the census, national accounts (GDP), labour market statistics, inflation figures, and population estimates.
beneficiaries: Government policy-makers, researchers, businesses, media, general public

name: HM Land Registry
description: Government department and trading fund that registers the ownership of land and property in England and Wales under the Land Registration Act 2002. Holds over 26 million titles and guarantees the title to registered estates and interests in land.
beneficiaries: Property owners, buyers, sellers, solicitors/conveyancers, mortgage lenders

name: Valuation Office Agency (VOA)
description: Executive agency of HMRC that provides valuations and property advice to the government, most notably compiling and maintaining the council tax valuation lists and non-domestic rating lists used to calculate business rates. Became an executive agency in 1991 but traces its origins to the Valuation Office established in 1910.
beneficiaries: Local authorities (for council tax and business rates), taxpayers, government departments requiring property valuations

name: National Savings and Investments (NS&I)
description: State-owned savings bank and executive agency of HM Treasury that offers savings and investment products backed by HM Treasury, tracing its origins to the Post Office Savings Bank of 1861. Raises cost-effective financing for the government through retail savings products including Premium Bonds, Income Bonds, and Direct Saver accounts.
beneficiaries: Individual savers seeking government-backed products, HM Treasury (as a source of government financing)

name: UK Debt Management Office (DMO)
description: Executive agency of HM Treasury responsible for debt and cash management for the UK government, including issuing gilts (government bonds) and Treasury bills to finance the government's borrowing requirement. Also manages the government's Exchequer cash position on a daily basis.
beneficiaries: HM Treasury, gilt market investors, financial institutions, UK taxpayers (through minimised financing costs)

name: Government Actuary's Department (GAD)
description: Non-ministerial department providing actuarial analysis, advice, and assurance to public sector clients across government on pensions, insurance, and social security matters. Advises on the financial sustainability of public service pension schemes, National Insurance Fund projections, and insurance regulation.
beneficiaries: Government departments, public sector pension schemes, regulators, public sector workers (indirectly)

name: Crown Estate
description: Independent commercial business established by the Crown Estate Act 1961, managing a £16 billion property portfolio belonging to the monarch in right of the Crown, including central London real estate, the seabed around England, Wales, and Northern Ireland, and rural estates. Net revenue profits are paid to the Treasury (and partly fund the Sovereign Grant) rather than being retained by the monarch.
beneficiaries: HM Treasury (revenue profits), offshore wind developers (seabed leasing), tenants, the Sovereign Grant

name: Forestry Commission / Forestry England
description: Non-ministerial government department responsible for protecting and expanding England's forests, established by the Forestry Act 1919 in response to timber shortages during World War I. Forestry England (rebranded from Forest Enterprise England in 2019) manages the nation's 1,500 public forests covering approximately 250,000 hectares.
beneficiaries: General public (recreation and amenity), timber industry, biodiversity and the environment, rural communities

name: Rural Payments Agency (RPA)
description: Executive agency of the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) that administers rural payment schemes in England, including the post-Brexit Environmental Land Management schemes (ELMs) that are replacing the EU's Common Agricultural Policy Basic Payment Scheme. Also responsible for land mapping, trader services, and rural inspections.
beneficiaries: Farmers and land managers in England, rural businesses, the environment (via agri-environment schemes)

name: Health Education England (now NHS England Workforce)
description: Responsible for workforce planning, education and training of NHS staff across England. Merged into NHS England in 2023 to consolidate workforce development functions.
beneficiaries: NHS workforce, medical trainees, healthcare students, patients (indirectly)

name: NHS Blood and Transplant
description: Manages the national blood supply for England and organ donation/transplantation services across the UK. Collects approximately 1.5 million blood donations annually and maintains the NHS Organ Donor Register.
beneficiaries: Patients requiring blood transfusions, organ transplant recipients, donors

name: NHS Digital (now NHS England Transformation)
description: Provided national IT infrastructure, data and digital services for the health and care system, including the NHS Spine and electronic health records. Merged into NHS England in 2023 as part of the Transformation Directorate.
beneficiaries: NHS trusts, GP practices, patients using digital health services, health researchers

name: Care Quality Commission (CQC)
description: Independent regulator of health and social care services in England, inspecting hospitals, care homes, GP surgeries and dental practices. Rates providers and has powers to enforce improvements or close services.
beneficiaries: Patients, care home residents, service users, the general public

name: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE)
description: Produces evidence-based guidance on clinical practice, public health and health technologies for the NHS. Appraises new medicines and treatments to determine cost-effectiveness and recommends whether the NHS should fund them.
beneficiaries: NHS clinicians, patients, commissioners, pharmaceutical companies (through appraisal process)

name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)
description: Regulates medicines, medical devices and blood components for transfusion in the UK, ensuring they meet safety, quality and efficacy standards. Responsible for granting marketing authorisations and monitoring adverse reactions through the Yellow Card scheme.
beneficiaries: Patients, pharmaceutical and medical device companies, healthcare professionals

name: Food Standards Agency
description: Independent government department responsible for food safety and food hygiene across England, Wales and Northern Ireland. Oversees food labelling, standards enforcement and the national food hygiene rating scheme.
beneficiaries: Consumers, food businesses, local authorities

name: Animal and Plant Health Agency
description: Executive agency of Defra responsible for safeguarding animal and plant health, preventing the spread of animal diseases such as avian influenza and bovine TB. Carries out surveillance, diagnostic testing and scientific research.
beneficiaries: Farmers, livestock keepers, plant growers, the general public

name: Veterinary Medicines Directorate
description: Executive agency of Defra that regulates the manufacture, marketing and post-authorisation surveillance of veterinary medicines in the UK. Ensures medicines for animals are safe, effective and manufactured to high quality standards.
beneficiaries: Animal owners, veterinary professionals, farmers, pharmaceutical companies

name: Health and Safety Executive (HSE)
description: National regulator for workplace health and safety in Great Britain, responsible for enforcing health and safety legislation and reducing work-related death, injury and ill health. Inspects workplaces, investigates incidents and provides guidance to employers.
beneficiaries: Workers, employers, the general public

name: Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration Service (ACAS)
description: Provides free and impartial advice to employers and employees on workplace relations and employment law. Offers conciliation services for employment disputes, including mandatory early conciliation before tribunal claims.
beneficiaries: Employees, employers, trade unions

name: Employment Tribunals
description: Judicial body that hears claims relating to employment law, including unfair dismissal, discrimination, equal pay and redundancy disputes. Operates as part of HM Courts and Tribunals Service across England, Wales and Scotland.
beneficiaries: Employees, employers, claimants and respondents in employment disputes

name: Gangmasters and Labour Abuse Authority
description: Regulates businesses that supply workers in agriculture, horticulture, shellfish gathering and associated processing and packaging. Investigates labour exploitation and modern slavery across all sectors using police-style enforcement powers granted in 2017.
beneficiaries: Vulnerable and exploited workers, legitimate labour providers

name: Low Pay Commission
description: Independent body that advises the government on the National Minimum Wage and National Living Wage rates. Comprises employer, worker and independent representatives who analyse economic evidence to recommend pay floor levels.
beneficiaries: Low-paid workers, employers, government

name: Equality and Human Rights Commission
description: National equality body for England, Scotland and Wales, responsible for enforcing equality legislation and protecting human rights. Monitors and promotes equality across the protected characteristics defined in the Equality Act 2010.
beneficiaries: Individuals facing discrimination, marginalised groups, employers, public bodies

name: Information Commissioner's Office (ICO)
description: Independent authority that upholds information rights in the public interest, enforcing the UK GDPR, Data Protection Act 2018 and Freedom of Information Act 2000. Has powers to issue fines, conduct audits and investigate data breaches.
beneficiaries: Individuals (data subjects), organisations processing personal data, FOI requesters

name: Charity Commission
description: Registrar and regulator of charities in England and Wales, responsible for ensuring charities operate for the public benefit and in accordance with charity law. Maintains the public register of charities and investigates mismanagement or misconduct.
beneficiaries: Charity trustees, donors, beneficiaries of charitable organisations, the public

name: Electoral Commission
description: Independent body that oversees elections and regulates political finance in the UK, ensuring elections are well-run and compliant with the law. Registers political parties, monitors campaign spending and sets standards for electoral administration.
beneficiaries: Voters, political parties, candidates, electoral administrators

name: Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman
description: Investigates complaints about UK government departments, agencies and NHS bodies in England that have not been resolved through their own complaints procedures. Makes recommendations for redress including apologies, explanations and financial compensation.
beneficiaries: Citizens with unresolved complaints against government or NHS services

name: National Audit Office (NAO)
description: Independent parliamentary body that audits central government departments, agencies and public bodies, reporting to the House of Commons on the economy, efficiency and effectiveness of public spending. Headed by the Comptroller and Auditor General.
beneficiaries: Parliament, taxpayers, government departments (through improvement recommendations)

name: Government Legal Department
description: Ministerial department providing legal advice and litigation services to most government departments and agencies across England and Wales. Successor to the Treasury Solicitor's Department, it is one of the largest legal organisations in the country.
beneficiaries: Government departments, ministers, public bodies

name: Treasury Solicitor
description: Historic office now part of the Government Legal Department, the Treasury Solicitor acts as head of the GLD and provides legal representation for the Crown. Also administers bona vacantia (ownerless property) in England and Wales on behalf of the Crown.
beneficiaries: The Crown, government departments, claimants on bona vacantia estates

name: Crown Commercial Service
description: Executive agency of the Cabinet Office that manages commercial agreements and procurement frameworks on behalf of the public sector, achieving savings through aggregated buying power. Manages over GBP 30 billion of public procurement annually.
beneficiaries: Government departments, NHS trusts, local authorities, public sector suppliers

name: Government Property Agency
description: Executive agency of the Cabinet Office responsible for managing the government's office estate across England, consolidating property holdings and creating modern shared workplaces. Aims to reduce the government estate footprint and save taxpayer money.
beneficiaries: Civil servants, government departments, taxpayers

name: Government Digital Service (GDS)
description: Part of the Cabinet Office responsible for the digital transformation of government, maintaining GOV.UK and setting digital standards across the public sector. Develops platforms including GOV.UK Notify, GOV.UK Pay and the GOV.UK Design System.
beneficiaries: Citizens accessing government services online, government departments, civil servants

name: Central Digital and Data Office
description: Cabinet Office unit responsible for the government's digital, data and technology strategy, setting cross-government standards and overseeing major digital programmes. Leads on data sharing, digital identity and the government's technology spending controls.
beneficiaries: Government departments, digital professionals in government, citizens

name: UK Statistics Authority
description: Independent body that oversees the Office for National Statistics and monitors the production and use of official statistics across the UK. Has statutory authority to assess official statistics against the Code of Practice for Statistics.
beneficiaries: Policymakers, researchers, the public, ONS staff

name: Boundary Commission for England
description: Independent body that reviews and recommends changes to parliamentary constituency boundaries in England, ensuring roughly equal voter numbers per constituency. Conducts periodic reviews as required under the Parliamentary Constituencies Act.
beneficiaries: Voters, political parties, Parliament

name: Office of the Public Guardian
description: Executive agency of the Ministry of Justice that registers lasting powers of attorney and deputyship orders, protecting people who lack mental capacity. Supervises deputies appointed by the Court of Protection and investigates concerns about attorneys or deputies.
beneficiaries: Vulnerable adults lacking mental capacity, their families, attorneys, deputies

name: Official Receiver / Insolvency Service
description: Executive agency of the Department for Business and Trade that administers compulsory company liquidations and personal bankruptcies, and investigates the conduct of bankrupt individuals and company directors. Maintains public confidence in the insolvency regime.
beneficiaries: Creditors, bankrupt individuals, employees of insolvent companies, the public

name: Companies House
description: Executive agency that incorporates and dissolves companies, registers company information and makes it publicly available on its online register. Holds records on over 5 million companies in the UK, with expanded verification powers under the Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023.
beneficiaries: Businesses, investors, lenders, law enforcement, the public

name: Intellectual Property Office
description: Executive agency of the Department for Business and Trade responsible for granting patents, registering trademarks and designs, and shaping UK intellectual property policy. Operates on a trading fund basis, largely self-financing through fees.
beneficiaries: Inventors, businesses, trademark holders, creative industries

name: UK Space Agency
description: Executive agency of the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology responsible for UK civil space policy, managing the UK's contribution to the European Space Agency and supporting the domestic space sector. Oversees satellite launch regulation and space sustainability.
beneficiaries: Space industry companies, researchers, satellite operators, the public

name: Ordnance Survey
description: National mapping agency for Great Britain, providing authoritative geospatial data and mapping products. Operates as a government-owned company and trading fund, generating revenue from data licensing and consumer products.
beneficiaries: Government agencies, businesses, emergency services, consumers, developers

name: Met Office
description: UK's national weather and climate service, providing weather forecasts, severe weather warnings and climate science research. Operates as an executive agency and trading fund of the Ministry of Defence, running some of the world's most powerful supercomputers for weather modelling.
beneficiaries: General public, emergency services, aviation, defence, agriculture, energy sector

name: UK Hydrographic Office
description: Produces nautical charts and hydrographic data for mariners worldwide under the ADMIRALTY brand, supporting safe navigation at sea. Operates as a trading fund of the Ministry of Defence, generating commercial revenue from data products.
beneficiaries: Mariners, shipping companies, Royal Navy, port authorities, offshore energy sector

name: Defence Infrastructure Organisation
description: Part of the Ministry of Defence responsible for building, maintaining and managing the defence estate across the UK, including military bases, training areas and housing. Manages approximately 1.8% of the UK land mass.
beneficiaries: Armed forces personnel, military families, MoD, defence contractors

name: Armed Forces Covenant Fund Trust
description: Independent charity that distributes funding from the Armed Forces Covenant Fund to programmes supporting the armed forces community across the UK. Awards grants for projects addressing veterans' needs including mental health, housing and integration.
beneficiaries: Veterans, serving personnel, military families, armed forces charities

name: War Pensions / Armed Forces Compensation
