description: Government schemes providing financial support to armed forces personnel and veterans injured or bereaved as a result of service. The War Pensions Scheme covers service before 6 April 2005, while the Armed Forces Compensation Scheme covers service from that date onwards.
beneficiaries: Injured veterans, bereaved military families, serving personnel with service-related conditions

name: Commonwealth War Graves Commission
description: Maintains the graves and memorials of 1.7 million Commonwealth servicemen and women who died in the First and Second World Wars, at 23,000 locations in over 150 countries. Funded jointly by six member governments with the UK contributing the largest share.
beneficiaries: Families of the war dead, veterans' organisations, the public, historians

name: British Transport Police
description: National police force responsible for policing railways, light rail systems and the London Underground across England, Wales and Scotland. Funded primarily by the rail industry through charges to train operating companies and Network Rail.
beneficiaries: Rail passengers, rail staff, train operating companies, the public

name: College of Policing
description: Professional body for policing in England and Wales, setting standards, developing training curricula and supporting evidence-based policing practice. Manages police officer recruitment assessments and the national Police National Database.
beneficiaries: Police officers, police forces, the public

name: Independent Office for Police Conduct (IOPC)
description: Oversees the police complaints system in England and Wales and independently investigates the most serious matters involving police officers. Successor to the Independent Police Complaints Commission, with enhanced powers to initiate investigations without referral.
beneficiaries: Complainants against police, police officers, the public

name: Fire and Rescue Service funding
description: Central government grant funding to fire and rescue authorities in England, supplementing council tax income to provide fire prevention, firefighting and rescue services. Distributed via the local government finance settlement alongside other Home Office grants.
beneficiaries: Local communities, fire and rescue authorities, firefighters

name: Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS)
description: Helps employers make safer recruitment decisions by processing criminal record checks and maintaining the barred lists of individuals unsuitable to work with children or vulnerable adults. Formed from the merger of the Criminal Records Bureau and the Independent Safeguarding Authority.
beneficiaries: Employers, children, vulnerable adults, job applicants

name: Gangmasters and Labour Abuse Authority
description: Duplicate of entry 106. Regulates gangmasters supplying workers in agriculture, horticulture and shellfish sectors, and investigates labour exploitation and modern slavery. Uses criminal enforcement powers to prosecute serious labour abuses.
beneficiaries: Vulnerable and exploited workers, legitimate labour providers

name: UK Anti-Doping Agency
description: National organisation responsible for protecting clean sport by implementing the UK's anti-doping policy in compliance with the World Anti-Doping Code. Conducts testing, education programmes and investigations into doping violations.
beneficiaries: Clean athletes, sports governing bodies, the sporting public

name: Sport England
description: Arms-length body of DCMS that invests National Lottery and government funding into grassroots and community sport and physical activity in England. Aims to increase participation and reduce inequalities in access to sport.
beneficiaries: Community sports clubs, local authorities, participants in sport and physical activity

name: UK Sport
description: Invests National Lottery and government funding in Olympic and Paralympic sports to support elite athletes and deliver medal success at major international competitions. Also supports bids to host major sporting events in the UK.
beneficiaries: Elite athletes, national governing bodies of sport, Olympic/Paralympic programmes

name: Arts Council England
description: National development agency for creativity and culture, distributing government and National Lottery funding to arts organisations, museums and libraries across England. Invests in a national portfolio of regularly funded organisations alongside project grants.
beneficiaries: Arts organisations, artists, museums, libraries, audiences, communities

name: British Film Institute (BFI)
description: Promotes and preserves film culture in the UK, distributing National Lottery funding for film production, distribution and education. Maintains the BFI National Archive, one of the largest film archives in the world, and operates BFI Southbank and BFI IMAX.
beneficiaries: Filmmakers, production companies, cinema audiences, film students, cultural heritage

name: English Heritage / Historic England
description: Historic England (the public body, established 2015) advises government on the historic environment, manages the heritage list and provides grants for conservation. English Heritage (the charity, separated in 2015) manages over 400 historic properties and sites.
beneficiaries: Heritage site visitors, property owners, local authorities, conservation professionals

name: British Museum
description: National museum housing a permanent collection of approximately 8 million objects spanning world history, art and culture, with free admission. One of the most visited museums globally, funded primarily by DCMS grant-in-aid alongside self-generated income.
beneficiaries: Museum visitors, researchers, students, the general public

name: Natural History Museum
description: National museum and leading scientific research institution with a collection of over 80 million specimens spanning 4.5 billion years of life and Earth history. Conducts globally significant research in biodiversity, planetary science and evolution.
beneficiaries: Museum visitors, scientists, students, conservation researchers

name: Victoria and Albert Museum
description: World's leading museum of art, design and performance, housing a permanent collection spanning 5,000 years of human creativity. Includes collections of fashion, textiles, ceramics, furniture, photography and digital design.
beneficiaries: Museum visitors, designers, artists, students, creative industries

name: Tate Galleries
description: National institution comprising Tate Modern, Tate Britain, Tate Liverpool and Tate St Ives, housing the national collection of British art and international modern and contemporary art. Tate Modern is one of the most visited modern art museums in the world.
beneficiaries: Gallery visitors, artists, art students, researchers, the public

name: National Gallery
description: Houses the national collection of Western European paintings from the 13th to 19th centuries, with over 2,300 works including masterpieces by Van Gogh, Turner and Leonardo. Offers free admission and is situated in Trafalgar Square, London.
beneficiaries: Gallery visitors, art students, researchers, the general public

name: British Library
description: National library of the United Kingdom and one of the largest research libraries in the world, holding over 170 million items including books, manuscripts, maps and sound recordings. Receives a copy of every publication produced in the UK and Ireland through legal deposit.
beneficiaries: Researchers, students, businesses, the general public

name: National Lottery Community Fund (formerly Big Lottery Fund)
description: Largest funder of community activity in the UK, distributing approximately GBP 600 million of National Lottery funding annually to community and voluntary organisations. Supports projects focused on community development, wellbeing and reducing inequalities.
beneficiaries: Community groups, voluntary organisations, social enterprises, local communities

name: Homes England
description: Government's housing accelerator, providing funding, land and expertise to support housebuilding and regeneration across England. Successor to the Homes and Communities Agency, it manages the Affordable Homes Programme and Help to Build equity loan scheme.
beneficiaries: Homebuyers, housing associations, developers, local authorities, renters

name: Building Safety Regulator
description: Established within the Health and Safety Executive under the Building Safety Act 2022 to oversee the safety and performance of all buildings, with a particular focus on higher-risk residential buildings over 18 metres. Regulates building control professionals and manages the new gateway system for building work.
beneficiaries: Residents of high-rise buildings, leaseholders, building professionals, the public

name: Planning Inspectorate
description: Executive agency of DLUHC that processes planning and enforcement appeals, examines local plans and handles applications for nationally significant infrastructure projects. Provides an independent, impartial decision-making service on planning matters.
beneficiaries: Planning applicants, local authorities, developers, communities, infrastructure promoters

name: Ofsted
description: Office for Standards in Education, Children's Services and Skills inspects and regulates services providing education and skills for learners of all ages, and services caring for children and young people. Publishes inspection reports to inform parents and the public about the quality of provision.
beneficiaries: Parents, pupils, students, childcare users, schools, colleges

name: Ofcom
description: UK's communications regulator overseeing broadband, mobile, postal services, broadcasting and online safety. Regulates spectrum allocation, enforces broadcasting standards and holds new duties under the Online Safety Act 2023.
beneficiaries: Consumers, telecoms companies, broadcasters, internet users

name: Ofwat
description: Economic regulator of the water and sewerage sector in England and Wales, setting price limits, service standards and investment requirements for water companies through periodic price reviews. Holds companies to account on performance, leakage and environmental impact.
beneficiaries: Water bill payers, the environment, water companies

name: Ofgem
description: Office of Gas and Electricity Markets regulates the gas and electricity markets in Great Britain, protecting consumer interests and overseeing the energy price cap. Administers environmental schemes and regulates network companies' investment plans.
beneficiaries: Energy consumers, energy suppliers, network operators

name: Financial Conduct Authority (FCA)
description: Regulates the conduct of approximately 50,000 financial services firms and financial markets in the UK, and acts as prudential regulator for smaller firms. Self-funded through levies on regulated firms, not from public expenditure.
beneficiaries: Financial services consumers, investors, regulated firms, financial markets

name: Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA)
description: Part of the Bank of England responsible for the prudential regulation and supervision of banks, building societies, credit unions, insurers and major investment firms. Promotes the safety and soundness of the firms it regulates and the stability of the UK financial system.
beneficiaries: Bank depositors, insurance policyholders, financial system participants

name: Financial Ombudsman Service
description: Independent body that resolves disputes between consumers and financial services firms, providing a free alternative to the courts. Handles hundreds of thousands of complaints annually covering banking, insurance, investments and payment services.
beneficiaries: Consumers with financial services complaints, financial firms

name: Payment Systems Regulator
description: Economic regulator of the UK's payment systems, promoting competition, innovation and the interests of service users. Subsidiary of the FCA, it oversees systems such as Faster Payments, BACS and card payment networks.
beneficiaries: Consumers, businesses, payment service providers, fintechs

name: Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)
description: UK's primary competition and consumer protection authority, investigating mergers, enforcing competition law, conducting market studies and protecting consumers from unfair practices. Gained expanded post-Brexit powers to review mergers previously assessed by the European Commission.
beneficiaries: Consumers, businesses, competitive markets

name: Advertising Standards Authority
description: Independent self-regulatory body for the advertising industry in the UK, enforcing the Advertising Codes across broadcast and non-broadcast media. Industry-funded rather than taxpayer-funded, it adjudicates complaints and ensures adverts are legal, decent, honest and truthful.
beneficiaries: Consumers, advertisers, media owners

name: Office of Rail and Road (ORR)
description: Independent regulator of rail and road in Great Britain, monitoring Network Rail's performance and efficiency, enforcing health and safety on railways, and monitoring Highways England's management of the strategic road network. Formerly the Office of Rail Regulation.
beneficiaries: Rail passengers, freight operators, road users, Network Rail, train companies

name: Transport for London (TfL) government grants
description: Central government provides capital and operating grants to Transport for London to support the Tube, buses, Overground, DLR and road network across London. Grant levels have varied significantly, with extraordinary funding deals agreed during and after the COVID-19 pandemic.
beneficiaries: London commuters, bus and Tube users, London businesses, TfL

name: Crossrail/Elizabeth line residual costs
description: Residual government funding for the Elizabeth line (Crossrail), which opened in stages in 2022 after significant cost overruns from the original GBP 14.8 billion budget. Covers final completion works, defects liability and financing costs from additional borrowing.
beneficiaries: Elizabeth line passengers, London and South East commuters, the rail network

name: Northern Powerhouse Rail
description: Strategic transport programme to improve east-west rail connectivity across the North of England, linking major cities including Leeds, Manchester, Sheffield and Liverpool. Scope was significantly scaled back in the 2021 Integrated Rail Plan compared to original proposals.
beneficiaries: Northern England commuters, businesses, northern city regions

name: Great British Railways (transition)
description: Proposed public body to integrate rail track and train operations under a single national leadership, replacing the current fragmented franchise system. The Great British Railways Transition Team operates pending legislation, taking over some functions from the former Rail Delivery Group.
beneficiaries: Rail passengers, freight operators, train operating companies, railway workers

name: UK Infrastructure Bank
description: Government-owned policy bank headquartered in Leeds, providing loans and equity finance to support private infrastructure investment in clean energy, transport and digital connectivity. Established with GBP 22 billion of financial capacity to crowd in private capital.
beneficiaries: Infrastructure developers, clean energy projects, local authorities, private investors

name: British Business Bank
description: Government-owned economic development bank that supports smaller businesses to access finance through guarantees, loans and venture capital programmes. Manages schemes including Start Up Loans, the Enterprise Finance Guarantee and the Future Fund.
beneficiaries: SMEs, start-ups, entrepreneurs, venture capital funds

name: Innovate UK
description: UK's national innovation agency, part of UK Research and Innovation, providing grants, loans and support to businesses developing new products, processes and services. Runs programmes such as the Knowledge Transfer Network and Catapult centres.
beneficiaries: Innovative businesses, start-ups, universities, research institutions

name: Research England
description: Council within UK Research and Innovation responsible for distributing block grant research and knowledge exchange funding to English universities, including Quality-Related (QR) research funding based on the Research Excellence Framework. Created as part of the UKRI structure under the Higher Education and Research Act 2017.
beneficiaries: English universities, researchers, postgraduate students

name: Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC)
description: Largest of the seven UKRI research councils by budget, funding research and training in engineering and the physical sciences including mathematics, computer science, materials and manufacturing. Supports approximately one-third of all publicly funded research in the UK.
beneficiaries: University researchers, PhD students, engineering and physical sciences communities

name: Medical Research Council (MRC)
description: UKRI research council funding biomedical and clinical research to improve human health, with a portfolio spanning molecular science, global health, neuroscience and population studies. Operates research units and institutes including the Laboratory of Molecular Biology in Cambridge.
beneficiaries: Medical researchers, clinicians, patients (through translational research), PhD students

name: Natural Environment Research Council (NERC)
description: UKRI research council funding environmental science research across atmospheric, earth, biological, hydrological and marine sciences. Operates the British Antarctic Survey, British Geological Survey and National Oceanography Centre.
beneficiaries: Environmental scientists, ecologists, climate researchers, PhD students

name: Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council (BBSRC)
description: UKRI research council investing in bioscience research and training spanning food security, industrial biotechnology, agriculture and world-class fundamental bioscience. Funds strategically important research institutes including Rothamsted Research and the Roslin Institute.
beneficiaries: Bioscience researchers, agriculture sector, food industry, PhD students

name: Science and Technology Facilities Council (STFC)
description: UKRI research council that operates and provides access to large-scale scientific facilities including the Diamond Light Source, ISIS Neutron and Muon Source, and major telescopes. Supports particle physics, nuclear physics, astronomy and space science research.
beneficiaries: Physicists, astronomers, facility users, PhD students, international research collaborations

name: Economic and Social Research Council (ESRC)
description: UKRI research council funding economic and social science research, including major longitudinal studies and data infrastructure. Supports research addressing societal challenges including productivity, inequality, wellbeing and public policy.
beneficiaries: Social scientists, economists, policymakers, PhD students

name: Arts and Humanities Research Council (AHRC)
description: UKRI research council funding research and postgraduate training in the arts and humanities, from history and languages to design and creative practice. Supports heritage science, cultural understanding and creative economy research.
beneficiaries: Arts and humanities researchers, PhD students, creative industries, cultural sector

name: Alan Turing Institute
description: UK's national institute for data science and artificial intelligence, headquartered at the British Library. Founded by five universities and backed by EPSRC funding, it conducts research in AI, data science, and their applications to real-world problems.
beneficiaries: AI and data science researchers, industry partners, government, PhD students

name: Francis Crick Institute
description: Europe's largest biomedical research institute, located in central London, conducting discovery research to understand the fundamental biology underlying health and disease. Funded by the MRC, Cancer Research UK and the Wellcome Trust as a partnership.
beneficiaries: Biomedical researchers, postdoctoral scientists, PhD students, patients (long-term)

name: Joint Nature Conservation Committee
description: Public body that advises the UK government and devolved administrations on nature conservation, providing evidence and scientific advice on UK-wide and international conservation issues. Established under the Environmental Protection Act 1990.
beneficiaries: Government, devolved nature conservation bodies, wildlife, the environment

name: Marine Management Organisation
description: Executive NDPB of Defra responsible for marine planning, licensing and enforcement in English waters, implementing the UK Marine and Coastal Access Act 2009. Manages marine protected areas, fishing quotas and marine development consents.
beneficiaries: Fishing industry, marine developers, coastal communities, marine environment

name: Canal & River Trust
description: Charity that manages and maintains 2,000 miles of canals and rivers in England and Wales, formerly managed by British Waterways. Receives a declining government grant alongside self-generated income from property, utilities and fundraising.
beneficiaries: Boaters, towpath users, waterside communities, wildlife, heritage visitors

name: DEPARTMENT FOR WORK AND PENSIONS (GBP297.4B)
description: The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) is the UK government department responsible for welfare, pensions, and child maintenance policy. It administers the state pension, Universal Credit, disability benefits, and employment support programmes.
beneficiaries: Pensioners, unemployed individuals, disabled persons, low-income families, and all UK residents eligible for social security benefits.

name: Department for Work and Pensions (GBP296.4B)
description: Core departmental spending line for DWP, covering its direct operational costs and programme expenditure for welfare and pension administration. This is the department-level entity within OSCAR as distinct from the total departmental group which includes arms-length bodies.
beneficiaries: Pensioners, benefit claimants, jobseekers, disabled individuals, and families receiving welfare support.

name: DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH (GBP214.2B)
description: The Department of Health (now Department of Health and Social Care) is the UK government department responsible for health and adult social care policy in England. It oversees the NHS, public health, and health-related research funding.
beneficiaries: All residents of England accessing NHS services, social care users, healthcare workers, and public health beneficiaries.

name: 10.2 Old age; of which: pensions (GBP143.2B)
description: COFOG (Classification of Functions of Government) category for state pension expenditure, covering the basic State Pension and new State Pension payments to retirees. This is the single largest welfare spending line in the UK budget.
beneficiaries: Men and women above State Pension age (currently 66) who have made sufficient National Insurance contributions.

name: DEPARTMENT FOR EDUCATION (GBP140.3B)
description: The Department for Education (DfE) is responsible for children's services and education policy in England, including early years, schools, higher and further education, and apprenticeships. It was re-established in 2010 from the former Department for Children, Schools and Families.
beneficiaries: Children, students, teachers, schools, academies, universities, apprentices, and families in England.

name: NHS Trusts (GBP135.5B)
description: NHS Trusts are public sector bodies established to provide hospital and community health services within the National Health Service. They manage hospitals, ambulance services, mental health services, and community health services across England.
beneficiaries: All patients and residents in England accessing hospital, ambulance, mental health, and community health services.

name: Department for Education (GBP132.9B)
description: Core departmental spending line for the DfE, representing direct programme and administrative expenditure on education policy, school funding, and children's services. Distinguished from the wider departmental group total which includes academies and other bodies.
beneficiaries: Schools, pupils, students, teachers, and educational institutions in England.

name: MINISTRY OF DEFENCE (GBP71.9B)
description: The Ministry of Defence (MoD) is the UK government department responsible for implementing defence policy, managing the Armed Forces, and procuring military equipment. It covers the Royal Navy, British Army, and Royal Air Force.
beneficiaries: UK Armed Forces personnel, veterans, defence industry contractors, and the UK population through national security.

name: Ministry of Defence (GBP71.4B)
description: Core departmental spending line for the MoD, covering military operations, personnel costs, equipment procurement, and estate management. This represents the department-level entity within OSCAR.
beneficiaries: Armed Forces personnel, defence civilians, veterans, and defence contractors.

name: 10.7 Social exclusion n.e.c.; of which: family benefits, income support and tax credits (GBP71.3B)
description: COFOG category covering means-tested benefits and tax credits aimed at reducing social exclusion, including Universal Credit, income-based Jobseeker's Allowance, Housing Benefit, and legacy tax credits. This captures welfare spending not classified under other social protection subcategories.
beneficiaries: Low-income families, unemployed persons, housing benefit claimants, and individuals receiving means-tested welfare support.

name: 2.1 Military defence (GBP67.9B)
description: COFOG category for military defence expenditure covering the administration, operation, and support of the UK Armed Forces including land, naval, and air forces, plus engineering, transport, and communications. Excludes military aid to foreign countries and R&D.
beneficiaries: UK Armed Forces personnel and the general public through national defence and security.

name: HM TREASURY (GBP63.5B)
description: HM Treasury is the UK's economics and finance ministry, responsible for fiscal policy, public expenditure control, and financial regulation. Its spending includes debt interest payments, government banking, and fiscal transfers.
beneficiaries: The UK government as a whole, financial markets, public sector bodies receiving central funding, and taxpayers.

name: 4.1 General economic, commercial and labour affairs (GBP63.0B)
description: COFOG category covering government spending on general economic policy, trade regulation, labour market programmes, and commercial affairs. Includes business support schemes, employment programmes, and economic development initiatives.
beneficiaries: Businesses, employers, workers, jobseekers, and the broader UK economy.

name: NHS Commissioning Board (NHS England) (GBP62.9B)
description: NHS England (formerly the NHS Commissioning Board) is the executive non-departmental public body that commissions primary care and specialist health services in England. Established under the Health and Social Care Act 2012, it allocates the majority of NHS funding to clinical commissioning groups and providers.
beneficiaries: All NHS patients in England, GP practices, hospitals, and health service providers receiving commissioned funding.

name: HM Treasury (GBP62.1B)
description: Core departmental spending line for HM Treasury, covering its direct programme expenditure including debt management, government banking, and fiscal policy administration. Distinguished from the departmental group total.
beneficiaries: UK government departments, public sector bodies, bondholders, and the general public.

name: 10.1 Sickness and disability; of which: incapacity, disability and injury benefits (GBP57.6B)
description: COFOG category for spending on disability and sickness benefits including Personal Independence Payment (PIP), Disability Living Allowance (DLA), Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), and industrial injuries benefits. One of the largest welfare spending categories.
beneficiaries: People with disabilities, those with long-term health conditions, individuals unable to work due to illness, and industrial injury claimants.

name: 9.5 Education not definable by level (GBP50.0B)
description: COFOG category for education expenditure that cannot be assigned to a specific level (primary, secondary, tertiary), including cross-cutting education programmes, school support services, and local authority education spending. Includes much of the Dedicated Schools Grant.
beneficiaries: Students across all education levels, schools, teachers, and education support staff.

name: SCOTLAND OFFICE AND OFFICE OF THE ADVOCATE GENERAL (GBP48.6B)
description: The Scotland Office represents Scottish interests within the UK Government and facilitates the devolution settlement. This spending line includes the block grant transfer to the Scottish Government under the Barnett formula.
beneficiaries: The Scottish Government, Scottish public services, and residents of Scotland.

name: Scotland Office and Office of the Advocate General (GBP48.6B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for the Scotland Office, which manages the constitutional relationship between UK and Scottish governments and transfers block grant funding. The Office of the Advocate General provides legal advice on Scots law matters to the UK Government.
beneficiaries: The Scottish Government, Scottish legal system, and the people of Scotland.

name: MINISTRY OF HOUSING, COMMUNITIES AND LOCAL GOVERNMENT (GBP46.1B)
description: MHCLG (now the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities) is responsible for housing policy, local government funding, community cohesion, and building safety in England. It administers major funding streams to local authorities.
beneficiaries: Local authorities, housing associations, tenants, homeowners, communities, and local government workers in England.

name: DEPARTMENT FOR TRANSPORT (GBP41.2B)
description: The Department for Transport (DfT) is responsible for transport policy in England and some UK-wide transport matters including aviation and maritime. It oversees roads, railways, buses, aviation, and shipping policy.
beneficiaries: Transport users, commuters, freight operators, road users, rail passengers, and transport infrastructure workers.

name: 9.4 Tertiary education (GBP40.7B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on universities, higher education institutions, and tertiary education including student loans, teaching grants, and research funding. Includes both resource and capital expenditure on higher education.
beneficiaries: University students, higher education institutions, researchers, and academic staff.

name: Academies (GBP40.5B)
description: Academies are publicly funded independent schools in England that receive funding directly from central government rather than through local authorities. They operate outside local authority control but are overseen by the DfE and regulated by Ofsted.
beneficiaries: Pupils attending academy schools, academy trust staff, and families in communities served by academies.

name: DfE Grants to Academies Offset (GBP37.0B)
description: An OSCAR consolidation adjustment entry that offsets the double-counting of grants from the Department for Education to Academies. Since academies are within the DfE departmental group, this negative entry eliminates intra-group transfers in consolidated accounts.
beneficiaries: Not directly applicable — this is an accounting consolidation entry to ensure accurate total spending figures.

name: SCOTTISH GOVERNMENT (GBP36.8B)
description: The Scottish Government is the devolved government for Scotland, responsible for health, education, justice, rural affairs, housing, and the environment in Scotland. It receives block grant funding from the UK Government via the Barnett formula plus devolved tax revenues.
beneficiaries: All residents of Scotland accessing devolved public services including health, education, and local government.

name: Scottish Government (GBP36.8B)
description: Core spending entity for the Scottish Government within OSCAR, reflecting the devolved administration's expenditure on public services in Scotland. Covers the full range of devolved policy areas.
beneficiaries: Scottish residents, Scottish public sector workers, and users of devolved services in Scotland.

name: MHCLG Local Government (GBP34.4B)
description: The local government funding arm of MHCLG, responsible for distributing Revenue Support Grant, business rates retention, and other grants to local authorities in England. This covers the main central government funding streams for councils.
beneficiaries: Local councils in England, council tax payers, and users of local government services including social care, waste collection, and planning.

name: HM REVENUE AND CUSTOMS (GBP34.3B)
description: HMRC is the UK's tax authority responsible for collecting taxes, paying tax credits and child benefit, and enforcing customs regulations. It was formed in 2005 from the merger of the Inland Revenue and HM Customs and Excise.
beneficiaries: Taxpayers receiving tax credits and child benefit, businesses interacting with tax and customs systems, and the Exchequer through revenue collection.

name: HM Revenue & Customs (GBP34.3B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for HMRC within OSCAR, covering administration of the tax system, payment of tax credits and child benefit, and customs enforcement operations.
beneficiaries: Tax credit recipients, child benefit claimants, taxpayers, importers/exporters, and the UK Exchequer.

name: NORTHERN IRELAND EXECUTIVE (GBP33.2B)
description: The Northern Ireland Executive is the devolved government of Northern Ireland, responsible for transferred matters including health, education, agriculture, enterprise, and the environment. It receives block grant funding from the UK Government.
beneficiaries: All residents of Northern Ireland accessing devolved public services.

name: Northern Ireland Executive (GBP32.9B)
description: Core spending entity for the Northern Ireland Executive within OSCAR, covering devolved spending on health, education, infrastructure, agriculture, and other transferred policy areas in Northern Ireland.
beneficiaries: Northern Ireland residents, public sector workers, and users of devolved services.

name: Not known (GBP30.0B)
description: An OSCAR classification for spending where the designated body or organisation is not identified or not yet allocated to a specific entity. This commonly occurs with legacy data, cross-cutting programmes, or expenditure pending proper classification.
beneficiaries: Various — cannot be determined as the spending entity is unclassified.

name: n/a (GBP28.3B)
description: A placeholder entry in OSCAR where a specific sub-classification or segment is not applicable to the spending line. This typically appears when a COFOG or organisational breakdown does not apply to a particular expenditure category.
beneficiaries: Various — depends on the parent spending category to which this unclassified amount belongs.

name: NORTHERN IRELAND OFFICE (GBP25.5B)
description: The Northern Ireland Office (NIO) is the UK Government department responsible for Northern Ireland affairs, including the constitutional position of Northern Ireland and the block grant transfer. It oversees political and security matters and the devolution settlement.
beneficiaries: The Northern Ireland Executive, Northern Ireland residents, and political institutions in Northern Ireland.

name: Northern Ireland Office (GBP25.5B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for the NIO, including the block grant to the Northern Ireland Executive, legacy issues, and political affairs management.
beneficiaries: Northern Ireland residents, the Northern Ireland Executive, and legacy inquiries participants.

name: n/a (GBP25.4B)
description: A not-applicable placeholder in OSCAR data where a particular classification dimension does not apply. Appears across various departmental and functional breakdowns.
beneficiaries: Various — cannot be specified without the parent spending context.

name: 9.8 Education n.e.c. (GBP25.1B)
description: COFOG category for education spending not elsewhere classified, covering education administration, subsidiary services to education (transport, meals, medical services for students), and other education support that does not fall into specific level categories.
beneficiaries: Students, education administrators, school support staff, and users of education subsidiary services.

name: Not known (GBP24.7B)
description: Another instance of unclassified spending in OSCAR where the specific body or sub-function has not been determined. May reflect data quality issues or transitional classification during departmental restructuring.
beneficiaries: Various — cannot be determined.

name: n/a (GBP24.3B)
description: Not-applicable classification entry in OSCAR, appearing where a breakdown dimension is irrelevant to the particular spending stream. Common in aggregated or departmental-level figures.
beneficiaries: Various — depends on parent category.

name: WELSH ASSEMBLY GOVERNMENT (GBP23.9B)
description: The Welsh Government (formerly Welsh Assembly Government) is the devolved government for Wales, responsible for health, education, local government, transport, and economic development in Wales. It receives block grant funding via the Barnett formula.
beneficiaries: All residents of Wales accessing devolved public services including NHS Wales, Welsh schools, and local government.

name: Welsh Assembly Government (GBP23.8B)
description: Core spending entity for the Welsh Government within OSCAR, covering devolved expenditure across health, education, housing, transport, and economic development in Wales.
beneficiaries: Welsh residents, Welsh public sector workers, and users of devolved services in Wales.

name: DEPARTMENT FOR ENERGY SECURITY AND NET ZERO (GBP22.1B)
description: DESNZ was created in February 2023 to handle energy policy, achieving net zero emissions, and energy security. It took over energy and climate functions from the former Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (BEIS).
beneficiaries: Energy consumers, renewable energy developers, households receiving energy efficiency support, and climate/environment stakeholders.

name: WALES OFFICE (GBP20.5B)
description: The Wales Office (Office of the Secretary of State for Wales) represents Welsh interests within the UK Government and manages the devolution settlement with the Welsh Government. Its spending includes the block grant transfer to Wales.
beneficiaries: The Welsh Government, Welsh public services, and residents of Wales.

name: Wales Office (GBP20.5B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for the Wales Office within OSCAR, primarily comprising the block grant transfer to the Welsh Government under the Barnett formula.
beneficiaries: The Welsh Government, Welsh public services, and the people of Wales.

name: n/a (GBP20.5B)
description: Not-applicable classification placeholder in OSCAR data where a specific sub-breakdown is not relevant to the spending line in question.
beneficiaries: Various — depends on parent spending category.

name: Not known (GBP20.3B)
description: Unclassified spending entry in OSCAR where the specific organisation or function has not been identified or allocated. Reflects gaps in classification or pending data reconciliation.
beneficiaries: Various — cannot be determined.

name: 7.A Medical services (GBP19.0B)
description: COFOG sub-category for expenditure on medical services including hospital services, GP services, dental care, and paramedical services. Covers the delivery of clinical healthcare to patients through the NHS and other providers.
beneficiaries: NHS patients, hospital users, GP patients, and all residents accessing medical care in the UK.

name: Network Rail (GBP17.9B)
description: Network Rail is the public body that owns and manages most of the railway infrastructure in Great Britain, including tracks, signals, tunnels, bridges, and stations. It was created to replace the failed Railtrack and is classified as a central government body.
beneficiaries: Rail passengers, train operating companies, freight operators, and communities served by the rail network.

name: HOME OFFICE (GBP17.4B)
description: The Home Office is the UK Government department responsible for immigration, security, counter-terrorism, policing, and drugs policy. It also oversees passports, the fire service, and border control.
beneficiaries: UK residents through policing and security, immigrants and asylum seekers, passport holders, and emergency services personnel.

name: Home Office (GBP17.3B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for the Home Office within OSCAR, covering immigration enforcement, border security, counter-terrorism, policing grants, and passport services.
beneficiaries: UK residents, police forces, border force staff, immigration applicants, and passport holders.

name: CABINET OFFICE (GBP15.8B)
description: The Cabinet Office supports the Prime Minister and the Cabinet, coordinates government policy, and manages the civil service. It also handles government efficiency, digital transformation, and national security coordination.
beneficiaries: Government ministers, civil servants, public sector workers, and the public through improved government efficiency and coordination.

name: 10.6 Housing (GBP14.9B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on housing, including Housing Benefit (now largely within Universal Credit), social housing provision, housing subsidies, and homelessness programmes.
beneficiaries: Social housing tenants, Housing Benefit recipients, homeless individuals, and first-time buyers benefiting from housing schemes.

name: Infected Blood Compensation Authority (GBP14.8B)
description: The Infected Blood Compensation Authority was established to deliver compensation to victims of the contaminated blood scandal of the 1970s-1990s, following the Infected Blood Inquiry recommendations. It administers payments to those infected with HIV and Hepatitis C through NHS blood products.
beneficiaries: Victims of the contaminated blood scandal, their families, and bereaved partners/dependants.

name: 10.4 Family and children; of which: family benefits, income support and tax credits (GBP14.6B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on family and children-related benefits including Child Benefit, child elements of Universal Credit, and legacy child tax credits. Covers cash transfers and services specifically targeted at families with children.
beneficiaries: Families with children, low-income parents, carers, and children receiving welfare support.

name: Department of Health (NHS) (GBP14.3B)
description: A specific OSCAR spending entity representing the Department of Health's direct NHS-related expenditure, distinct from NHS Trusts and NHS England. Covers central NHS programme costs, public health, and departmental health policy implementation.
beneficiaries: NHS service users, public health beneficiaries, and healthcare system participants in England.

name: MINISTRY OF JUSTICE (GBP14.3B)
description: The Ministry of Justice (MoJ) is responsible for the courts, prisons, probation, and legal aid in England and Wales. It was created in 2007, taking over functions from the former Department for Constitutional Affairs and parts of the Home Office.
beneficiaries: Court users, prisoners, probation clients, legal aid recipients, victims of crime, and the legal profession.

name: DEPARTMENT FOR SCIENCE, INNOVATION AND TECHNOLOGY (GBP14.2B)
description: DSIT was created in February 2023 to drive UK science, research, innovation, and technology policy. It took over science, research, and digital functions from BEIS and DCMS, and oversees UK Research and Innovation (UKRI).
beneficiaries: Researchers, scientists, technology companies, universities, innovators, and digital infrastructure users.

name: 3.1 of which: other police services (GBP14.1B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on police services excluding immigration-related policing, covering grants to police forces, police operations, and law enforcement activities across England and Wales.
beneficiaries: Police forces, police officers, communities benefiting from law enforcement, and crime victims.

name: Ministry of Justice (GBP14.0B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for the MoJ within OSCAR, covering courts administration (HMCTS), prison service (HMPPS), probation, and legal aid programme costs.
beneficiaries: Court users, prisoners, probation service users, legal aid recipients, and justice system professionals.

name: Nuclear Decommissioning Authority (GBP13.5B)
description: The NDA is a non-departmental public body responsible for the decommissioning and clean-up of the UK's civil nuclear legacy sites, including Sellafield, Dounreay, and former Magnox power stations. It manages the UK's most complex and hazardous nuclear waste.
beneficiaries: Local communities near nuclear sites, nuclear industry workers, environmental protection, and future generations through safe waste management.

name: 1.6 General public services n.e.c. (GBP13.4B)
description: COFOG category for general public services spending not elsewhere classified, including public debt transactions (interest payments), general government transfers between levels, and services that cannot be assigned to specific functions.
beneficiaries: Various — primarily relates to government debt holders (through interest payments) and cross-government service users.

name: DEPARTMENT FOR CULTURE, MEDIA AND SPORT (GBP13.2B)
description: DCMS (now Department for Culture, Media and Sport) is responsible for culture, arts, media, sport, tourism, civil society, and the creative industries. It oversees bodies including the BBC, Arts Council, Sport England, and the National Lottery.
beneficiaries: Arts organisations, sports bodies, cultural institutions, media companies, tourists, creative industry workers, and civil society organisations.

name: FOREIGN, COMMONWEALTH AND DEVELOPMENT OFFICE (GBP12.5B)
description: The FCDO was formed in 2020 by merging the Foreign and Commonwealth Office with the Department for International Development. It manages the UK's diplomatic relations, overseas aid (ODA), consular services, and development programmes worldwide.
beneficiaries: UK citizens abroad, developing countries receiving UK aid, diplomatic personnel, and international organisations.

name: Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (GBP12.4B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for the FCDO within OSCAR, covering diplomatic operations, overseas development assistance, consular services, and international programme spending.
beneficiaries: Developing countries, UK nationals abroad, international organisations, and diplomatic staff.

name: 7.A Medical services (GBP11.6B)
description: A second COFOG sub-entry for medical services spending, likely representing a different segment or departmental allocation of clinical healthcare expenditure (e.g., devolved or locally delivered medical services distinct from the main NHS entry).
beneficiaries: NHS patients and healthcare users accessing clinical medical services.

name: 4.1 General economic, commercial and labour affairs (GBP10.9B)
description: A second instance of this COFOG category, likely representing a different departmental or segmental allocation of economic affairs spending such as business support, labour market interventions, or commercial regulation.
beneficiaries: Businesses, workers, employers, and economic development bodies.

name: UK Research and Innovation (GBP10.0B)
description: UKRI is the national funding body for research and innovation, created by the Higher Education and Research Act 2017. It brings together seven Research Councils, Innovate UK, and Research England into a single strategic body directing research funding.
beneficiaries: Universities, research institutions, scientists, innovators, businesses conducting R&D, and doctoral students.

name: n/a (GBP9.7B)
description: Not-applicable classification entry in OSCAR where a specific sub-breakdown or organisational dimension does not apply to this spending stream.
beneficiaries: Various — depends on parent spending category.

name: 5.1 Waste management (GBP9.7B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on waste collection, treatment, and disposal services, including household waste, recycling, hazardous waste management, and nuclear waste cleanup (which accounts for a large share via the NDA).
beneficiaries: Households, local authorities, waste management companies, and communities benefiting from environmental waste services.

name: CABINET OFFICE: CIVIL SUPERANNUATION (GBP9.6B)
description: The Civil Superannuation scheme provides pensions for civil servants and other eligible public sector workers. Managed through the Cabinet Office, it covers the Principal Civil Service Pension Scheme and associated pension liabilities.
beneficiaries: Retired civil servants, current civil service pension scheme members, and dependants of deceased civil servants.

name: Cabinet Office: Civil Superannuation (GBP9.6B)
description: Core spending entity for the Civil Superannuation pension scheme within OSCAR, covering pension payments to retired civil servants and scheme administration costs.
beneficiaries: Retired and serving civil servants, pension scheme members, and their dependants.

name: TEACHERS' PENSION SCHEME (ENGLAND AND WALES) (GBP9.3B)
description: The Teachers' Pension Scheme is the occupational pension scheme for teachers in England and Wales, administered by the Department for Education. It is one of the largest public sector pension schemes in the UK.
beneficiaries: Retired teachers, serving teachers contributing to the scheme, and dependants of deceased teachers in England and Wales.

name: Teachers' Pension Scheme (England & Wales) (GBP9.3B)
description: Core spending entity for the Teachers' Pension Scheme within OSCAR, covering pension payments, lump sum retirement benefits, and scheme administration for the teaching workforce.
beneficiaries: Retired and serving teachers, education support staff eligible for the scheme, and their dependants.

name: Department for Transport (GBP9.3B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for the DfT within OSCAR, covering direct programme expenditure on transport policy, road and rail investments, local transport grants, and aviation and maritime regulation.
beneficiaries: Transport users, commuters, road users, local transport authorities, and freight operators.

name: 10.2 Old age; of which: pensions (GBP9.2B)
description: A second COFOG entry for old age pension spending, likely representing a different segment such as public sector occupational pensions (civil service, teachers, NHS, armed forces) distinct from the State Pension.
beneficiaries: Public sector retirees receiving occupational pensions and their dependants.

name: 7.C Central and other health services (GBP9.0B)
description: COFOG sub-category covering central health administration, public health services, health policy and regulation, and health services not classified as medical services or medical research. Includes health system management and public health programmes.
beneficiaries: Public health beneficiaries, health system administrators, and the general population through disease prevention and health promotion.

name: 1.2 Foreign economic aid (GBP8.9B)
description: COFOG category for Official Development Assistance (ODA) and other foreign economic aid spending, covering bilateral and multilateral aid programmes, humanitarian assistance, and contributions to international development organisations.
beneficiaries: Developing countries, international aid organisations, humanitarian aid recipients, and global development programme participants.

name: DEPARTMENT FOR ENVIRONMENT, FOOD AND RURAL AFFAIRS (GBP8.9B)
description: Defra is the UK Government department responsible for environmental protection, food production and standards, agriculture, fisheries, and rural communities. It oversees bodies including the Environment Agency, Natural England, and the Rural Payments Agency.
beneficiaries: Farmers, rural communities, food industry workers, environmental organisations, and the public through environmental protection and food safety.

name: MHCLG Communities (GBP8.3B)
description: The communities arm of MHCLG, covering spending on community development, homelessness prevention, building safety, integration, and local community programmes. Distinct from the local government funding stream.
beneficiaries: Communities, homeless individuals, housing associations, building residents, and community organisations in England.

name: Bristish Broadcasting Corporation (GBP7.7B)
description: The BBC is the UK's national public service broadcaster, funded primarily through the television licence fee. In OSCAR it appears as a public sector body, with spending covering TV, radio, online services, and the World Service.
beneficiaries: UK television and radio audiences, World Service listeners globally, and the creative industries workforce.

name: 3.3 Law courts (GBP7.3B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on the court system including criminal, civil, and family courts, tribunals, and judicial services. Covers HM Courts & Tribunals Service operations, judicial salaries, and legal aid for court proceedings.
beneficiaries: Court users, litigants, defendants, judges, legal professionals, and victims seeking justice through the court system.

name: 7.A Medical services (GBP7.2B)
description: A third COFOG entry for medical services, representing another segmental allocation of healthcare spending — possibly covering a specific devolved administration or functional sub-segment of clinical service delivery.
beneficiaries: Patients and healthcare users accessing clinical medical services.

name: National Highways (GBP7.0B)
description: National Highways (formerly Highways England and before that the Highways Agency) is the government company responsible for operating, maintaining, and improving England's strategic road network — motorways and major A-roads.
beneficiaries: Motorists, freight operators, road users, and communities along the strategic road network in England.

name: High Speed 2 (GBP6.9B)
description: HS2 Ltd is the company responsible for developing and building the High Speed 2 railway, intended to connect London with the Midlands and the North. It is one of Europe's largest infrastructure projects, though its northern phases have been scaled back.
beneficiaries: Future rail passengers, construction workers, communities along the route, and the broader economy through improved connectivity.

name: 9.1 of which: under fives (GBP6.8B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on early years education and childcare for children under five, including free childcare entitlements, nursery provision, and Sure Start children's centres.
beneficiaries: Children under five, their parents/carers, nursery providers, childminders, and early years educators.

name: 4.8 R&D economic affairs (GBP6.8B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on research and development related to economic affairs, including industrial research, technology development, and innovation funding through bodies like Innovate UK and research councils focused on economic applications.
beneficiaries: Research institutions, businesses conducting R&D, scientists, engineers, and technology innovators.

name: 3.4 Prisons (GBP6.7B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on the prison system, covering the operation of prisons, young offender institutions, and immigration removal centres, as well as probation and rehabilitation services managed by HMPPS.
beneficiaries: Prisoners undergoing rehabilitation, prison staff, probation workers, and society through public protection and offender rehabilitation.

name: 10.2 Old age; of which: pensions (GBP6.0B)
description: A third COFOG entry for old age pensions, likely representing another specific public sector pension scheme allocation or a different accounting segment of pension expenditure.
beneficiaries: Pensioners receiving public sector or state pension payments.

name: 1.1 Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs (GBP5.5B)
description: COFOG category covering spending on the executive (government departments), legislative organs (Parliament), financial and fiscal affairs (Treasury, HMRC), and external diplomatic affairs. Includes core government administration costs.
beneficiaries: Government departments, Parliament, diplomatic services, and the public through effective governance and fiscal management.

name: 1.6 General public services n.e.c. (GBP5.3B)
description: A second instance of this COFOG category for general public services not elsewhere classified, potentially representing a different departmental or functional allocation of cross-cutting government service expenditure.
beneficiaries: Various public service users and government operations.

name: 10.9 Social protection n.e.c. (GBP5.1B)
description: COFOG category for social protection spending not elsewhere classified, covering miscellaneous welfare programmes, social fund payments, and other social support that does not fit into specific subcategories like pensions, disability, or family benefits.
beneficiaries: Individuals receiving miscellaneous social support including crisis loans, funeral payments, and other welfare assistance.

name: 10.1 Sickness and disability; of which: incapacity, disability and injury benefits (GBP5.0B)
description: A second COFOG entry for sickness and disability benefit spending, likely representing a different departmental allocation or segment of disability-related expenditure such as devolved or locally administered disability support.
beneficiaries: People with disabilities, those with long-term health conditions, and incapacity benefit claimants.

name: 10.2 Old age; of which: pensions (GBP4.8B)
description: A fourth COFOG entry for old age pension spending, representing yet another pension scheme or segment — possibly NHS pensions, local government pensions, or pension credit expenditure.
beneficiaries: Pensioners and retirees receiving pension payments from public sector schemes.

name: ARMED FORCES PENSION AND COMPENSATION SCHEMES (GBP4.8B)
description: The Armed Forces Pension Scheme and Armed Forces Compensation Scheme provide retirement pensions and injury compensation to serving and former members of the UK Armed Forces. The compensation scheme replaced the War Pensions scheme for service after 2005.
beneficiaries: Retired military personnel, injured veterans, serving Armed Forces members, and dependants of deceased service personnel.

name: Armed Forces Pension and Compensation Schemes (GBP4.8B)
description: Core spending entity for military pensions and compensation within OSCAR, covering pension payments to retired military personnel and compensation for service-attributable injuries or death.
beneficiaries: Military retirees, veterans with service-related injuries, war widows/widowers, and Armed Forces dependants.

name: 3.6 Public order and safety n.e.c. (GBP4.5B)
description: COFOG category for public order and safety spending not elsewhere classified, covering fire services, civil defence, emergency planning, and other protective services not included in police, courts, or prisons categories.
beneficiaries: Fire service personnel, communities protected by emergency services, and civil defence beneficiaries.

name: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (GBP4.3B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for Defra within OSCAR, covering direct programme expenditure on environmental policy, agricultural support, rural development, and food standards.
beneficiaries: Farmers, rural communities, environmental protection beneficiaries, and food industry participants.

name: 4.5 of which: railway (GBP4.2B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on railway transport, covering rail infrastructure investment (Network Rail), rail subsidies, franchise support, and railway capital projects including new lines and upgrades.
beneficiaries: Rail passengers, train operating companies, rail freight operators, and railway workers.

name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (GBP4.0B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for DSIT within OSCAR, covering direct expenditure on science policy, research funding, digital infrastructure, and technology regulation.
beneficiaries: Researchers, technology companies, digital infrastructure users, and innovation ecosystem participants.

name: 8.3 Broadcasting and publishing services (GBP3.9B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on broadcasting and publishing, primarily covering the BBC licence fee funding, S4C (Welsh language broadcasting), and other public service broadcasting support.
beneficiaries: BBC viewers and listeners, S4C audience, public service broadcasting staff, and the creative industries.

name: n/a (GBP3.9B)
description: Not-applicable classification placeholder in OSCAR where a specific dimensional breakdown does not apply to the spending line.
beneficiaries: Various — depends on parent spending category.

name: n/a (GBP3.8B)
description: Not-applicable classification entry in OSCAR for a spending line where a particular sub-breakdown dimension is irrelevant.
beneficiaries: Various — depends on parent spending category.

name: 6.2 Community development (GBP3.6B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on community development programmes, including neighbourhood renewal, community regeneration, levelling up funds, and local community support initiatives.
beneficiaries: Disadvantaged communities, local regeneration areas, community organisations, and residents of areas receiving development funding.

name: 10.1 Sickness and disability; of which: incapacity, disability and injury benefits (GBP3.6B)
description: A third COFOG entry for sickness and disability spending, representing another segment of disability and incapacity benefit expenditure across government departments or devolved administrations.
beneficiaries: Disabled individuals and those with long-term sickness receiving benefits.

name: 7.A Medical services (GBP3.5B)
description: A fourth COFOG entry for medical services, representing another segmental allocation of clinical healthcare expenditure across the UK — possibly a specific devolved nation's health service spending.
beneficiaries: Patients accessing medical services within the relevant jurisdiction.

name: Not known (GBP3.3B)
description: Unclassified spending entry in OSCAR where the responsible body or functional classification has not been determined or allocated.
beneficiaries: Various — cannot be determined.

name: 9.2 Secondary education (GBP3.2B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on secondary education (ages 11-16/18), covering secondary school funding, teacher costs, curriculum delivery, and school facilities for secondary-age pupils.
beneficiaries: Secondary school pupils, teachers, school staff, and families with secondary-age children.

name: Office for Students (GBP3.2B)
description: The Office for Students (OfS) is the independent regulator for higher education in England, established under the Higher Education and Research Act 2017. It distributes teaching funding to universities and ensures quality and value for money in higher education.
beneficiaries: University students in England, higher education institutions, and prospective students through quality assurance and access initiatives.

name: 4.1 General economic, commercial and labour affairs (GBP3.1B)
description: A third instance of this COFOG category, representing another departmental or segmental allocation of spending on economic policy, commercial regulation, and labour market programmes.
beneficiaries: Businesses, workers, and economic policy beneficiaries.

name: 1.1 Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs (GBP3.1B)
description: A second instance of this COFOG category for core government administration spending, likely representing a different departmental allocation of executive, legislative, or fiscal affairs expenditure.
beneficiaries: Government institutions, parliamentary operations, and public through effective governance.

name: 9.1 of which: primary education (GBP2.9B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on primary education (ages 5-11), covering primary school funding, teacher costs, and school facilities for primary-age pupils.
beneficiaries: Primary school pupils, teachers, school staff, and families with primary-age children.

name: 10.2 Old age; of which: pensions (GBP2.8B)
description: A fifth COFOG entry for old age pension spending, representing another specific pension scheme or administrative segment within the broader old age pension expenditure.
beneficiaries: Pensioners and retirees receiving payments from the relevant pension scheme.

name: 9.7 R&D education (GBP2.8B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on research and development in the education sector, covering educational research, pedagogical innovation, and R&D conducted within educational institutions funded by government.
beneficiaries: Education researchers, universities conducting educational R&D, teachers benefiting from pedagogical research, and students.

name: 2.4 R&D defence (GBP2.8B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on defence-related research and development, covering military technology development, defence science programmes (through DSTL), and MOD-funded R&D contracts with industry.
beneficiaries: Defence science and technology organisations, military R&D contractors, Armed Forces through improved capabilities, and DSTL staff.

name: 10.7 Social exclusion n.e.c.; of which: family benefits, income support and tax credits (GBP2.7B)
description: A second COFOG entry for social exclusion spending, representing another segment of means-tested welfare expenditure aimed at preventing social exclusion through income support and tax credits.
beneficiaries: Low-income households, unemployed individuals, and families receiving means-tested benefits.

name: DEPARTMENT FOR BUSINESS AND TRADE (GBP2.7B)
description: DBT was created in February 2023, taking over business, trade, and industrial strategy functions from the former BEIS and the Department for International Trade. It covers trade policy, export promotion, business regulation, and competition policy.
beneficiaries: UK businesses, exporters, importers, trade partners, and workers affected by trade and industrial policy.

name: 6.1 of which: other social housing (GBP2.7B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on social housing excluding housing development, covering housing management, maintenance of social housing stock, housing association support, and affordable housing programmes.
beneficiaries: Social housing tenants, housing associations, local authority housing departments, and people on social housing waiting lists.

name: 9.5 Education not definable by level (GBP2.7B)
description: A second COFOG entry for education spending not definable by level, representing another segment of cross-cutting education expenditure that cannot be attributed to primary, secondary, or tertiary levels.
beneficiaries: Students and educational institutions across multiple levels of education.

name: 9.1 of which: primary education (GBP2.5B)
description: A second COFOG entry for primary education spending, representing another departmental or segmental allocation of primary school funding — possibly a devolved administration's share.
beneficiaries: Primary school pupils, teachers, and schools.

name: Low Carbon Contracts Company (GBP2.5B)
description: The Low Carbon Contracts Company (LCCC) is a government-owned company that manages Contracts for Difference (CfDs) with low-carbon electricity generators, providing long-term price stability to incentivise investment in renewable energy generation in the UK.
beneficiaries: Renewable energy generators, wind farm and solar operators, electricity consumers through decarbonisation, and clean energy investors.

name: 4.8 R&D economic affairs (GBP2.5B)
description: A second COFOG entry for R&D in economic affairs, representing another allocation of government spending on economic and industrial research and development, likely from a different department or segment.
beneficiaries: Research institutions, businesses engaged in R&D, and innovation-focused organisations.

name: 9.4 Tertiary education (GBP2.2B)
description: A second COFOG entry for tertiary education spending, representing another segment of higher education expenditure — possibly covering a devolved administration or specific programme like student maintenance grants.
beneficiaries: University students, higher education institutions, and academic staff.

name: 3.1 of which: other police services (GBP2.2B)
description: A second COFOG entry for police services spending, representing another departmental or regional allocation of policing expenditure — possibly covering devolved police forces or specific policing programmes.
beneficiaries: Police forces, officers, and communities benefiting from policing services.

name: Department for Business and Trade (GBP2.1B)
description: Core departmental spending entity for DBT within OSCAR, covering direct programme expenditure on trade promotion, business support, export finance, and commercial regulation.
beneficiaries: UK businesses, exporters, trade negotiators, and business regulation stakeholders.

name: Sizewell C (Holding) Limited (GBP2.0B)
description: Sizewell C (Holding) Limited is a UK government holding company established to manage the government's investment in the Sizewell C nuclear power station project in Suffolk. The government took a stake to ensure the project proceeds as part of energy security strategy.
beneficiaries: UK energy consumers through future low-carbon baseload power, construction workers, nuclear industry, and Suffolk communities.

name: 9.2 Secondary education (GBP2.0B)
description: A second COFOG entry for secondary education spending, representing another allocation of funding for secondary schools — possibly a devolved administration or a specific programme segment.
beneficiaries: Secondary school pupils, teachers, and schools.

name: 5.6 Environment protection n.e.c. (GBP2.0B)
description: COFOG category for environmental protection spending not elsewhere classified, covering general environmental policy, biodiversity conservation, landscape protection, and environmental programmes not included in waste, pollution, or water categories.
beneficiaries: Environmental organisations, protected landscapes, wildlife, and the public through environmental conservation.

name: 7.B Medical research (GBP1.9B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on medical and health-related research, covering the Medical Research Council, National Institute for Health Research (NIHR), and other health R&D funding through universities and research institutions.
beneficiaries: Medical researchers, universities, research hospitals, pharmaceutical development, and patients benefiting from medical advances.

name: 4.2 of which: other agriculture, food and fisheries policy (GBP1.8B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on agriculture, food, and fisheries policy excluding CAP market support, covering domestic agricultural subsidies (post-Brexit environmental land management schemes), food standards, and fisheries management.
beneficiaries: Farmers, food producers, fishing communities, rural landowners, and food safety beneficiaries.

name: 5.3 Pollution abatement (GBP1.8B)
description: COFOG category for government spending on pollution abatement and control, covering air quality monitoring, water pollution prevention, soil contamination cleanup, and regulatory enforcement of pollution standards.
beneficiaries: Communities affected by pollution, environmental regulators, and the general public through cleaner air and water.

name: 4.5 of which: local public transport (GBP1.8B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on local public transport, covering bus subsidies (Bus Service Operators Grant), local transport grants, light rail, and local authority transport services outside of national rail.
beneficiaries: Bus passengers, local transport users, bus operators, local transport authorities, and communities dependent on public transport.

name: Sizewell C Limited (GBP1.7B)
description: Sizewell C Limited is the project company developing the Sizewell C nuclear power station, a proposed twin-reactor plant in Suffolk based on the EPR design used at Hinkley Point C. It sits under the holding company structure for the government's nuclear investment.
beneficiaries: UK energy consumers, nuclear industry workforce, construction sector, and Suffolk local economy.

name: 4.5 of which: railway (GBP1.7B)
description: A second COFOG entry for railway transport spending, representing another allocation of rail infrastructure or subsidy expenditure — possibly covering a specific programme or devolved rail funding.
beneficiaries: Rail passengers, train operators, and railway workers.

name: 4.5 of which: other transport (GBP1.7B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on transport modes not covered by road, rail, or local public transport categories, including aviation, maritime, ports, inland waterways, and multi-modal transport programmes.
beneficiaries: Aviation users, maritime and shipping industry, port authorities, and inland waterway users.

name: ROYAL MAIL STATUTORY PENSION SCHEME (GBP1.5B)
description: The Royal Mail Statutory Pension Scheme (RMSPS) was created when the UK Government assumed responsibility for Royal Mail's historic pension liabilities in 2012 as part of the privatisation process. The government took on approximately GBP28 billion in pension obligations.
beneficiaries: Former and retired Royal Mail workers whose pension liabilities were transferred to the government scheme.

name: Royal Mail Statutory Pension Scheme (GBP1.5B)
description: Core spending entity for the RMSPS within OSCAR, covering ongoing pension payments to retired Royal Mail employees whose pension was taken over by government during the Royal Mail privatisation.
beneficiaries: Retired Royal Mail employees and their dependants.

name: 10.4 Family and children; of which: family benefits, income support and tax credits (GBP1.5B)
description: A second COFOG entry for family and children spending, representing another segment of family-related benefit expenditure — possibly covering a devolved administration or specific family support programme.
beneficiaries: Families with children and low-income parents receiving family-related benefits.

name: Departmental unallocated provision (GBP1.5B)
description: An OSCAR classification for spending provision that has been allocated to a department but not yet assigned to a specific programme, body, or function. This represents budget headroom or reserves held centrally within a department for future allocation.
beneficiaries: Not directly applicable — this is a budget planning provision awaiting allocation to specific programmes.

name: 3.1 of which: immigration and citizenship (GBP1.3B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on immigration control, border management, asylum processing, visa services, and citizenship administration. Primarily covers Home Office immigration enforcement and UK Visas and Immigration operations.
beneficiaries: Visa applicants, asylum seekers, immigrants, border force personnel, and UK citizens through managed immigration.

name: 9.1 of which: primary education (GBP1.3B)
description: A third COFOG entry for primary education spending, representing another segment of primary school funding — possibly covering a different devolved administration or specific primary education programme.
beneficiaries: Primary school pupils, teachers, and primary schools.

name: National Wealth Fund Limited (GBP1.3B)
description: The National Wealth Fund (formerly UK Infrastructure Bank, rebranded in 2024) is a government-owned institution providing finance for infrastructure and clean energy projects. It invests in projects supporting the UK's net zero and economic growth objectives.
beneficiaries: Infrastructure developers, clean energy projects, local authorities, and communities benefiting from infrastructure investment.

name: 4.1 General economic, commercial and labour affairs (GBP1.2B)
description: A fourth instance of this COFOG category, representing another departmental or segmental allocation of economic affairs spending such as labour market programmes, business regulation, or economic development.
beneficiaries: Businesses, workers, and economic development beneficiaries.

name: 4.2 of which: market support under CAP (GBP1.2B)
description: COFOG sub-category for government spending on agricultural market support under the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) or its UK successor schemes post-Brexit. Covers direct payments to farmers, market intervention, and agricultural price support.
beneficiaries: Farmers, agricultural landowners, and food producers receiving direct payments or market support.

name: 9.2 Secondary education (GBP1.2B)
description: A third COFOG entry for secondary education spending, representing another allocation of secondary school funding — possibly from a devolved administration or specific programme.
beneficiaries: Secondary school pupils, teachers, and schools.

name: 10.9 Social protection n.e.c. (GBP1.2B)
description: A second COFOG entry for social protection spending not elsewhere classified, representing another segment of miscellaneous welfare expenditure such as social fund, war pensions, or other non-categorised social support.
beneficiaries: Individuals receiving miscellaneous social protection support.

name: Arts Council (GBP1.2B)
description: Arts Council England is the national development agency for creativity and culture, distributing public money and National Lottery funding to arts and cultural organisations. Originally established as the Arts Council of Great Britain in 1946.
beneficiaries: Arts organisations, museums, theatres, artists, cultural venues, creative workers, and audiences engaging with publicly funded arts.

name: Not known (GBP1.1B)
description: Unclassified spending entry in OSCAR where the responsible organisation or functional category has not been determined or remains unallocated.
beneficiaries: Various — cannot be determined.

name: 3.1 of which: other police services (GBP1.1B)
description: A third COFOG entry for police services spending, representing another departmental or regional allocation of policing expenditure — potentially from a devolved administration or specific policing programme.
beneficiaries: Police forces, officers, and communities benefiting from policing.
